title: Sydney ‘plyscraper’ and Atlassian’s new headquarters reaches new heights
text:
Advertisement

CultureArt & designCity life Sydney ‘plyscraper’ and Atlassian’s new headquarters reaches new heights Linda Morris July 22, 2026 — 12:04pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Some 39 storeys above Central Station, roads and rail lines radiate like scattered spaghetti between the Lego-like blocks of Sydney’s rooftops, extending to almost every point of the compass until meeting the blue horizon of ocean and harbour, and mountains. Far below, Mike Cannon-Brookes and his Atlassian co-founder Scott Farquhar were 25 years ago living in separate flat shares in Glebe and Randwick and building their software business. Up to half of the building could be leased to other businesses. Atlassian The physical manifestation of their ambitions, the $1.45 billion Atlassian Central tower, has structurally topped out. The core of what will be the world’s tallest hybrid timber building – dubbed the “plyscraper” – now stands at its highest point on the city skyline, built from a rare combination of mass timber, steel and concrete. The now solo tech entrepreneur joined NSW Treasurer Daniel Mookhey at the site on Wednesday morning to mark the milestone for Tech Central, Sydney’s technology precinct.

Advertisement

Loading “Sydney has always been our home and it’s one of our busiest offices anywhere, but in all these years we’ve never had a chance to build our own space from the ground up for how we work,” Cannon-Brookes said. As is traditional for topping-out ceremonies and as a gesture of good fortune for the build, an Australian native evergreen was lifted to the summit earlier this week to celebrate the placement of the final, highest structural beam. The tree recognises the work, skills and dedication of the tradespeople and construction crew and developers who brought the project to its maximum height. The landmark building officially eclipses the previous record holder, the 25-storey Ascent tower in Milwaukee in the United States, by 100 metres. Crown Towers is the city’s tallest habitable building at 272 metres. Mike Cannon-Brookes at the topping-out ceremony for the Atlassian building. James Brickwood

Advertisement

But those wings have been clipped along the way. Cannon-Brookes insists the software firm remains committed to its investment in its new headquarters despite recently reducing its workforce by 1600 employees. Related Article Exclusive

City life ‘TBC’: How $190 million was blown on Circular Quay revamp before the first sod is even turned Atlassian will serve as the chief anchor tenant with three of the habitats subleased to other tech ventures – a smaller footprint than first envisaged, but a move Atlassian says aligns with its mission to create a mecca for startups and innovators. “From the outset, we set a mission to have intelligence and sustainability baked into the building, not as an afterthought – which means better materials, better engineering, much better data and, in the end, a much better outcome for all concerned.” For property developer and co-owner Dexus, and builder Built, the next phase involves filling the steel exoskeleton with mass timber elements and a glass facade. Crews are installing central building services, with the development on track for practical completion by the end of this year.

Advertisement

Designed by local practice BVN in collaboration with New York-based firm SHoP Architects, the tower reconfigures traditional office tower spaces, where departments are confined to single floors on enclosed levels. BVN’s project director Tim Crawshaw said the building will extend to the top of a ring beam which is still to be cladded and varies in height due to the slope required for sun access into Prince Alfred Park. His collaborator Andrea Teixeira, associate for ShoP, said the design was seven years in development since its participation in the 2019 international design competition. Its global clients had been closely watching the project, especially the concept of the floating four floors of timber and its natural ventilation and landscape. Atlassian Central is the world’s tallest hybrid timber tower at more than 180 metres. James Brickwood She predicts this project will change people’s mind about working from home. “Here, they are connected to the city, they are not in a satellite village.” The new office spaces are arranged into interconnected four-storey clusters known as “habitats”, where three timber floors are arranged between steel-and-concrete mega floor plates.

Advertisement

Stacked within a surrounding steel exoskeleton – a structural approach similar to that of new Powerhouse Parramatta – these timber habitats eliminate the need for internal columns. Each pod features one fully air-conditioned floor and three floating lightweight timber floors attached to the steel-and-concrete core by revolutionary drag straps. These are recessed to overlook a triple-height atrium “park space” partially open to the elements. At street level, the base of the tower will incorporate a reconstructed historic Parcels Shed, which will be housing a 480-bed YHA youth hostel. In all, 10,000 cubic metres of timber, highly specialised and sourced from Austria, will go to the project, along with 8000 tonnes of structural steel and 30,000 cubic metres of green concrete. title: ‘I’m gonna die of this’: The hidden cost of former Socceroos captain’s greatest gift
text:
Advertisement

SportSoccerAustralian soccer ‘I’m gonna die of this’: The hidden cost of former Socceroos captain’s greatest gift Vince Rugari July 22, 2026 — 12:00pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

For as long as he can remember, former Socceroos captain Paul Wade has had a gift. When he was 11 years old, newly arrived in Australia from England, he realised he could head a football harder, cleaner and more accurately than the other kids he would play with. Paul Wade at his home on the Central Coast. Wolter Peeters “Goalkeepers always kicked the ball into the air back then. Nobody threw it out,” he said. “And because I was in midfield, I was probably one of the very few that was willing to head it. Right from the start, I knew I could head a ball - even if it was on the top of my head, where it hurt.

Advertisement

“I just felt that this was something I can do. And as I got older, the more I did it, the more accurate I got with my headers, the more people went: ‘Yeah, Wadey can head a ball. This is really cool.’ And then I just loved it.” Wade would go on to win 84 caps for the Socceroos, wear the captain’s armband more often than almost anyone, and build a reputation as one of Australia’s most fearless competitors. He retired in 1997, but continued to endear himself to generations of Australian soccer fans through his work in the media, becoming one of the game’s most recognisable faces and voices. Diego Maradona (right) and Socceroos captain Paul Wade walk onto the Sydney Football Stadium for the first leg of a World Cup qualifying tie in 1993. Getty Not that he thought much about it at the time, but Wade’s willingness to put his body - and his head - on the line was the foundation for his legendary career. Every time he was split open by a stray elbow in an aerial contest, Wade could feel the adulation from supporters, teammates and coaches, and it made him walk taller. “It was a badge of honour. And I fed off it,” he said.

Advertisement

“They cheered for me and they had respect for me when I went back into the dressing rooms. And for the rest of my career, I didn’t mind getting it done: stitches at halftime, coming off concussed and then saying, ‘Right boss, I’m ready to go back on.’” Wade, now 64, cites the motto that he and so many footballers of his vintage used to live by: never let them know you’re hurt. Former Socceroos captain Paul Wade has been diagnosed with probable CTE. Wolter Peeters “That’s what kept me out there,” he said. “And it didn’t matter what happened, who hit me, if I fell on somebody - I didn’t make it an issue.” Little did he know his greatest attribute would cause him so much grief later in life.

Advertisement

These days, Wade’s memory is not as good as it once was. It has never been great: he was diagnosed with epilepsy during his playing career and underwent brain surgery in 2002 to remove a scarred section of his temporal lobe, and that history made it easier for him to dismiss the concerns of those closest to him whenever he forgot an appointment, or couldn’t find something he had put down only 30 seconds earlier. Related Article Concussion crisis ‘My brain was smashed’: Knocked unconscious for nine minutes, this ex-Socceroo is living a nightmare But there were also changes to Wade’s behaviour which didn’t track with the person they knew. Eventually, those changes became serious enough for his family and friends to intervene. “There were a couple of incidents where Paul’s behaviour was not appropriate,” said his wife, Val. “And when I say not appropriate … he spoke to people and did things that were just not OK. We nearly lost a friendship over it. That was the turning point. That’s when we sat him down and said, ‘You’ve got to go and see a neurologist. There’s something wrong, this is not you.’

Advertisement

“It still took probably a year to get him to do it, because he still didn’t think that there was something wrong with him, and he just kept putting it off.” After speaking to his GP, Val began researching his symptoms and saw signs consistent with CTE, or chronic traumatic encephalopathy, a degenerative brain disease associated with prolonged exposure to repeated head impacts. Definitive CTE diagnoses can only be made after death. But late last year, Wade was told by Dr Rowena Mobbs, one of Australia’s leading concussion experts, that he had probable CTE - the highest level of diagnostic certainty possible in a living patient, based on his history as a footballer, neurological symptoms and specialist assessment. Wade can’t point to a single, major concussion incident which changed everything for him, like his former Socceroos teammate Warren Spink. But CTE is also associated with prolonged exposure to repeated head impacts, including sub-concussive blows that may not produce immediate or obvious symptoms. In football, that has focused attention on the cumulative effect of heading the ball over the course of a career, or lifetime. “When I found out, the girls [his daughters] were very concerned. I said, ‘Don’t worry. I haven’t changed. It’s just that they’ve given me a headline,’” Wade said.

Advertisement

Related Article Analysis

FIFA World Cup Spanish domination shows the limitations of ‘suffering’ in football “But when the neurologist said, ‘It’s terminal. Go and make some memories,’ that’s when reality kicks in. All my mates have been going, ‘Yeah, but we’re all terminal, Wadey.’ But I’ll never forget those words when she said them, that it’s terminal. “I’m gonna die of this. I might not die for 20 years, I might get hit by a bus, but there is no cure. And that was the moment I thought, people have got to understand … you don’t have to have a concussion to have CTE.” Wade doesn’t blame anyone and has no regrets. That’s just what he and so many others who loved football did, and still do. But he is hopeful that, by going public with his story, greater awareness of the dangers of heading the ball can be raised, and the risks can be mitigated. England has prohibited deliberate heading in matches for children aged under 12, while limiting heading in training for older junior age groups. Mobbs wants Australia to introduce similar restrictions, and Wade would also like to see that happen.

Advertisement

“I just want people to know that if you want to continue to do thousands of headers, these could be the consequences,” Wade said. “And for those who are making the rules or should be applying the protocols, do it. Do it now. Don’t think about the consequences of that if you can save a few thousand lives. “Because if I die in the next five years, all those doubters will go, ‘Wow, we’ve just wasted five years.’” Loading

title: Cruise boat captain sacked after sleeping in front of passengers
text:
Advertisement

BusinessCompaniesUnfair dismissal Cruise boat captain sacked after sleeping in front of passengers Millie Muroi July 22, 2026 — 11:59am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

A cruise tour boat captain has failed to win back his job after being sacked for unsafe and “cavalier” behaviour including sleeping and snoring in front of guests and leaving unqualified staff to steer the boat. The man, who worked for Queensland business SeaLink Whitsundays on their “Wildcat” vessels from June 2024 to June 2025, was employed as a casual master — responsible for the safe operation of the boat — and was found by the Fair Work Commission to have been validly dismissed. The cruise captain was found to have dumped food into the water while guests were snorkelling nearby. In June last year, one of SeaLink’s deckhands made a complaint about “very unsafe behaviour” from a “current skipper within the company”. The allegations included the man sleeping on the vessel and snoring in front of guests to the point where they asked “what’s wrong with the skipper?”, constantly vaping on board, and making other crew members drive the boat despite their lack of qualification.

Advertisement

Related Article Unfair dismissal ‘Evasive’ postie who drove to KFC on lunch break loses job Several other staff deckhands also gave evidence against the man. Fair Work Commission deputy president Nicholas Lake said that during the hearing, while the man was “perhaps not deliberately dishonest”, he often deflected and blamed others when asked about his own behaviour. “He appeared to enjoy the theatrics of being on the witness stand,” Lake said, adding the man tried to dismiss the evidence of the young women involved in the investigation, calling them “a junior group of girls who have nothing better to do than gossip”. The man also compared a girls-only group chat for women working on the boat to the movie Mean Girls.

Advertisement

Lake said the man’s suggestion that the witnesses were less reliable because they were allegedly gossipy young women was “condescending at best and sexist at worst” and noted he was the least credible witness of all the witnesses who gave evidence. The man admitted to falling asleep on multiple occasions but said it was common practice in some instances such as when the boat was moored and guests were on the beach, and that in another instance, he had taken sleeping tablets before his shift and in retrospect should not have gone to work that day. The man worked for Queensland business SeaLink Whitsundays on their “Wildcat” vessels from June 2024, was employed as a casual master Getty Images/iStockphoto The man also admitted he vaped on board occasionally, but said he did it to help him quit smoking after seeing a hypnotherapist. One deckhand alleged the man said, “I just don’t give a f---, let them die,” in response to a conversation about snorkel control measures, but he denied this and the commission found there was inadequate evidence to prove this had occurred.

Advertisement

Related Article Unfair dismissal Chauffeur who agreed to retire at 65 to be paid $40,000 after ageist dismissal The commission also said the man asked his deckhands to take the helm without supervision despite them not having the appropriate qualifications to drive the vessel, although the man argued he hadn’t put them “in control” of the boat. “They were following direct orders from me to drive in a straight line,” he said. “There were no manoeuvres, they were not asked to change direction, they were not asked to change engine speeds.” The man admitted he had thrown a tray of rice salad overboard during one trip out of frustration with the crew and acknowledged this was an error in judgment as it breached marine pollution regulations. However, one witness alleged it was a more significant amount of food including bread rolls, meats and salads, thrown into the water while guests were snorkelling, and that the man had said he did not want to carry heavy bins of food at the end of the day. The man argued that his conduct amounted to “a number of rather innocuous indiscretions”, but the commission ultimately found there was a pattern of inappropriate behaviour.

Advertisement

However, the commission noted there was some doubt about whether the man had a fair opportunity to respond to the company’s allegations before a conclusion had been reached on them and to have his say on whether the conduct warranted dismissal. “I consider the rushed and appended disciplinary process in this matter was unreasonable and, as a result, caused some unfairness,” Lake said, ordering $5000 in compensation to be paid to the man. title: Miners and energy stocks push ASX higher; Lynas, tech stocks decline
text:
Advertisement

Updated BusinessMarketsWorld markets Miners and energy stocks push ASX higher; Lynas, tech stocks decline Staff writers Updated July 22, 2026 — 11:58am ,first published July 22, 2026 — 5:13am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

The Australian sharemarket advanced in early trade on Wednesday after Wall Street was pushed higher by gains in makers of computer chips and other winners of the artificial-intelligence boom, shrugging off another jump in oil prices amid the hostilities in the Middle East. The S&P/ASX 200 was up 25.3 points, or 0.3 per cent, at 8818.60 as of 11.22am AEST, boosted by the energy and mining sectors. The local bourse closed flat on Tuesday. The Australian dollar was trading at US$70.07. AI stocks once again were at the centre of the action on Wall Street, and they rose for a second straight day after tumbling the week before. AP Energy and materials stocks pushed the ASX higher in early trade, thanks to higher prices for oil, copper and gold overnight. Brent crude oil briefly got near $US92 per barrel for the first time in more than five weeks because of continued attacks between the US and Iran. It later pared its gain to 2 per cent and settled at $US91.01. That’s up from less than $US72 in early July, which is roughly where it was before the war with Iran. US President Donald Trump played down the prospect of immediate talks with Iran as the two sides exchanged strikes near the Strait of Hormuz, while Houthi militants in Yemen threatened shipping in the Red Sea.

Advertisement

Local oil giants Woodside and Santos rose in early trade, up 0.6 and 0.8 per cent, respectively, while local refiners Ampol and Viva Energy both added 1.2 per cent. Coal producer Yancoal gained 1.7 per cent as higher oil prices boost demand for coal as a fossil fuel alternative. Mining heavyweights BHP, Rio Tinto and Fortescue jumped 3 per cent, 2.4 per cent and 2 per cent, respectively, after copper futures jumped 3.3 per cent. After demand for iron ore peaked, the mining giants have pivoted to the red metal to become their main earnings driver. Related Article Opinion

Online shopping $900 million fines look like the cost of doing business for giant shopping apps Elizabeth Knight Business columnist Meanwhile, gold prices held their gains as traders monitored the war-induced threats to energy supply routes that risk stoking inflation and putting pressure on the Federal Reserve to hike interest rates. Bullion was trading at about $US4080 an ounce, after gaining almost 2 per cent the previous session. Northern Star Resources rose 2.5 per cent, while Evolution Mining jumped 4.2 per cent. South32 climbed a further 3.7 per cent, adding to its 6.6 per cent rally on Tuesday. The mining outlier this morning was Lynas Rare Earths, which slumped 6.2 per cent after the company revealed in a trading update a more than 10 per cent slump in its output of NdPR – an important alloy blend of rare earths elements crucial for making the strong magnets that power the traction motors used in electric cars and wind turbines – in the June quarter compared to a year ago, due to issues at its new Mount Weld water recycling plants and ore concentrate quality issues.

Advertisement

While rising oil prices boosted energy stocks, the flip side is threatening a re-acceleration of inflation, which in turn could push the US Federal Reserve and other central banks to raise interest rates, slowing economies and undercutting prices for stocks and other investments. Real estate investment trusts, which tend to suffer when rising rates boost the returns of bonds, were trading lower, with warehouse and AI data centre owner Goodman Group down 1.2 per cent, Westfield shopping centres landlord Scentre down 0.5 per cent and Vicinity down 1.1 per cent. Healthcare stocks also declined, with biotech giant CSL down 1.9 per cent and Chemist Warehouse owner Sigma Healthcare slipping 1.42 per cent. Tech stocks gave back their gains from Tuesday, with software makers bearing the brunt of the losses. Xero, the nation’s biggest tech stock, lost 4 per cent. WiseTech fell 2 per cent after saying it bought a developer of AI-powered supply chain risk and compliance intelligence technology for $10 million and shares. Technology One slid 3 per cent. Wesfarmers slipped 0.7 per cent after the conglomerate said it would spend between $645 million and $715 million to expand the Mount Holland lithium mine and concentrator with its partner, Sociedad Quimica y Minera de Chile. It will pay for the investment using existing cash and debt facilities.

Advertisement

The big four banks were slightly higher in early trade. CBA was up 0.3 per cent, Westpac and National Australia Bank both rose 0.4 per cent and ANZ Bank edged up 0.1 per cent. Related Article Opinion

Trade wars Trump wants to make his trade wall bigger and better than ever before Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist On Wall Street overnight, the S&P 500 climbed 0.9 per cent. The Dow Jones added 385 points, or 0.7 per cent, and the Nasdaq composite rose 1.3 per cent. AI stocks once again were at the centre of the action, and they rose for a second straight day after tumbling the week before. After rocketing higher because of the boom in investment in AI chips and data centres, they’ve come under pressure in recent weeks on worries that they shot too high. Concerns are also weighing that investment in AI may fall off if it doesn’t produce as much profit as hoped.

Advertisement

Micron Technology jumped 12.2 per cent and added to its 1.9 per cent gain from the day before, coming off its 13.3 per cent drop from last week. Nvidia added 2 per cent; they were the two strongest forces lifting the S&P 500. On Wall Street, several stronger-than-expected profit reports from big US companies also helped the market higher. 3M climbed 7.3 per cent after topping analysts’ expectations for both profit and revenue in the latest quarter. It also raised its forecast for profit over the full year of 2026. Hasbro rallied 8.8 per cent after the toy maker said its Magic: The Gathering game topped $US500 million in revenue for a quarter for the first time. It also raised its revenue forecast for the year. General Motors cruised 4.9 per cent higher after the automaker’s profit and revenue for the latest quarter beat analysts’ expectations and CEO Mary Barra said demand in North America remains strong. Alphabet slid 1.5 per cent after Google released a trio of cheaper versions of its Gemini AI model, but shared no timing updates for the flagship Pro model that has already delayed its launch by several weeks.

Advertisement

title: The real oil shock from the Iran war might be just beginning
text:
Advertisement

BusinessMarketsMiddle East at war Opinion The real oil shock from the Iran war might be just beginning Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist July 22, 2026 — 11:57am

July 22, 2026 — 11:57am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

A new front has opened in the war in the Middle East, global oil and refined product inventories are tumbling and oil and gasoline prices are spiking again. So much for all that winning. After the 60-day ceasefire agreement was signed by the United States and Iran last month and the Strait of Hormuz reopened, shipments of crude oil from the Gulf recommenced and oil and gasoline prices fell sharply, with Brent crude trading (briefly) below $US70 a barrel. Maritime traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has ground to a halt yet again, raising concerns of another supply shock. AP The oil market, which had been helped by the release of about 300 million barrels from strategic reserves and China’s halving of its imports, appeared to be recovering rapidly. Global oil inventories rose by 21 million barrels in June, the first increase since the outbreak of the war in February. The breakdown of the ceasefire late last month initially had only a modest impact on oil prices, which edged up towards, and then above, $US80 a barrel as the hostilities resumed and intensified.

Advertisement

On Monday, however, with the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed again, the Iran-aligned Houthis emailed most of the world’s major shipping companies, threatening to attack ships attempting to export oil from Red Sea ports via the Bab el-Mandeb strait. The Saudis have been using their pipelines and their port at Yanbu to ship about 4 million barrels a day – compared with only about a million barrels a day before the war broke out – via the Red Sea, with about 2.5 million barrels a day heading south, via the Bab el-Mandeb, to Asian buyers and the rest travelling the far longer route via the Suez Canal (which can’t handle the larger tankers) at the northern end of the sea. The oil price immediately broke through $US90 a barrel. The world is now far more vulnerable than it was in the earlier phase of the war.

Advertisement

The International Energy Agency’s July oil market report said that about three quarters of the 400 million barrels of strategic oil reserves that its members agreed to release in March have been released. What’s left of that commitment could be exhausted within weeks. The oil shock definitely isn’t over and, indeed, with no obvious way for the US to gracefully retreat from the conflict it chose to start, the real shock might just be getting underway. The ceasefire did produce a surge in oil “on the water” for the first time since February, but it also produced a revival in demand, which had previously been reduced as the war pushed up prices. Both OECD and non-OECD oil stocks fell again last month – by a combined total of roughly 100 million barrels – and Washington’s strategic reserve has fallen to its lowest level in more than 40 years. More threatening is the position in refined products. Businesses and consumers don’t consume oil, but its derivatives like petrol, diesel jet fuels and fertilisers.

Advertisement

The margins on refined products hit four-year highs earlier this month, even when crude was trading just above $US70 a barrel. Exports from the Persian Gulf of refined products were less than half their prewar levels last month, even as crude flows were running at about 75 per cent of prewar levels again. That disparity is partly due to the combination of Iranian attacks on Middle East refineries and the depletion of their inventories. But stocks have also been hit by the success of Ukraine’s targeting of Russian refineries and oil industry infrastructure, which has forced Russia – previously the world’s second-largest exporter of diesel – not just to halt exports of diesel refined products, but to start importing them. That has subtracted supply and added demand to the market for refined products, as has a shift in China’s energy security strategy. During the war, China nearly halved its imports of crude, taking about 5 million barrels a day of demand out of the market while restricting exports of refined products.

Advertisement

With a strategic reserve of its own estimated at more than 1.2 billion barrels, it was better positioned than any other economy to ride out the war. With the ceasefire, however, it resumed purchases of oil to rebuild its depleted reserve. Related Article Updated

Middle East at war Trump to bomb Iran’s Pickaxe Mountain nuclear site ‘soon’, appears to blame Jordan for US deaths The released strategic reserves and China’s withdrawal from the market are probably the two major factors in oil prices failing to reach the $US150-a-barrel prices that were predicted early in the conflict, which the IEA described as the biggest energy shock in history. The price peaked at about $US126 a barrel in late April. Those buffers, however, have been run down and, with the Strait of Hormuz again closed and the Houthis threatening to shut the Bab el-Mandeb, supply is under an even more significant threat than it was during the first phase of the conflict. The most acute threat to the global economy remains the supply of refined product.

Advertisement

Gasoline and diesel inventories around the world are at or close to record lows – US diesel stocks are, for instance, at 20-year lows and gasoline stocks at their lowest level since 2012 – and global refining output, which didn’t surge like crude oil flows during the ceasefire, is around 5 million barrels a day lower than it was a year ago. An oil tanker struck by the Houthis in the Red Sea in 2024. The rebel group is threatening to strike tankers attempting to export oil from Red Sea ports via the Bab el-Mandeb strait. AP The US had boosted exports of refined products to record levels (with record profits for US refiners) as Middle East supply fell away, but the decline in domestic stocks has seen its refiners’ output redirected to the domestic market, with export volumes falling back quite sharply. With no obvious ability to bring back refined product supply to prewar levels quickly while the conflict continues – the damaged refineries in the Middle East and Russia will take months, at least, to repair and ramp up their output – it is demand destruction, driven by price spikes, that will have to drive a rebalancing of the supply-demand equation. Ominously for US President Donald Trump’s Republicans facing midterm elections in November, US petrol prices rose back above $US4 a gallon this week. This time last year, the average US petrol price was $US3.14 a gallon. Diesel is selling at about $US5.14 a gallon, compared to $US3.72 a year ago.

Advertisement

Related Article Oil These are the corporate gamblers who never waste a good energy crisis The oil shock – and more particularly the downstream effects of that shock on refined product prices and supply – definitely isn’t over and, indeed, with no obvious way for the US to gracefully retreat from the conflict it chose to start, the real shock might just be getting underway. That has implications for global economic activity and for inflation rates around the world. The drop in the US inflation last month from 4.2 per cent to 3.5 per cent as the ceasefire took hold and oil and gasoline prices fell illustrated how significant the impact of the war has been on businesses and households. Trump, of course, says he isn’t worried about the escalation and broadening of Middle East hostilities, even as he professed no interest in restarting negotiations with Iran.

Advertisement

And if the Houthis do close the Red Sea? “If something like that happens, we take care of it,” he said. “We’ll take care of it”: Donald Trump says he isn’t worried about the escalation and broadening of Middle East hostilities. AP The US has done such a great job of taking care of the Strait of Hormuz that less than a handful of tankers now risk the passage each day. “If we left tomorrow, we had a big, we had a big success. But we’re not leaving tomorrow,” Trump said this week. Trump defines success and winning differently to the rest of us.

Advertisement

Every day that the two straits remain closed and oil and refined product prices stay elevated underscores how unsuccessful – and humiliating – Trump’s excursion in the Middle East has been, and how costly it is – not just for the US, but for the rest of the world. title: Is it ever OK to yell at your dog? These experts have advice for Barnaby Joyce
text:
Advertisement

LifestyleLife & relationshipsPets Is it ever OK to yell at your dog? These experts have advice for Barnaby Joyce Kate Leaver July 22, 2026 — 11:48am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

On Monday evening, One Nation MP Barnaby Joyce interrupted his interview with ABC’s 7.30 host Sarah Ferguson to yell at his dog. In a clip that has since gone viral, he began to answer a question about One Nation leader Pauline Hanson’s “intolerance and racial discrimination” when he looked off-camera and bellowed: “Sit down”. “Sorry,” he then said, regaining composure. “It’s just the dog.” “Don’t worry about the dog,” Ferguson said. “Just answer the question.” But perhaps he should have worried a little more about his dog, a young staffy-border collie cross, named Stella. Because, according to experts, yelling isn’t necessarily the best approach when it comes to training your pet.

Advertisement

Want to teach an old dog new tricks? Don’t follow in Barnaby Joyce’s footsteps. Marija Ercegovac What a dog hears when you yell “Dogs love predictability and when their guardian is suddenly angry and shouty, they’re not learning to stop a behaviour, but instead learning that their person is scary and unpredictable,” says Cath Jennings, a certified professional dog trainer, behaviourist and veterinary nurse. “A single stressful event will flood a dog’s system with stress hormones and cortisol sticks around. There’s so much stress associated with living in our human world and many dogs never get a chance to really decompress. We, their family, should be one thing they can rely upon to make sense and to always be kind.” Joyce has since explained that Stella was underneath the deck of his home, barking incessantly.

Advertisement

Related Article Sleep Exactly how bad is it to sleep with your pet? Experts weigh in And while barking can be annoying to human ears, according to Dr Suzie Fowler, chief science officer at RSPCA Australia, a dog’s hearing is even more sensitive than ours, meaning shouting can be startling. “It’s not just the volume, though. The tone of voice, body language and overall emotion all contribute to how a dog experiences the interaction,” says Fowler. “In general, dogs respond much better to calm, consistent communication and positive reinforcement for good behaviour with those well-deserved treats.” What should dog owners do instead? Advice for the best way to train or treat a dog has changed over the years.

Advertisement

While there used to be a trend for punishment – telling them off, rubbing their faces in the mess they’ve made – but that approach is very much outdated. It is now more about patience, consistency, empathy and understanding what our dogs are trying to communicate. “Dogs are loving animals that give us so much joy and companionship, it is only fair that we repay them with equal respect and compassion. It is what the community expects and also what helps reinforce a positive relationship between you and your dog,” says Fowler. Loading A live television cross might not be the easiest moment to train a dog. Ideally, Joyce would have organised for Stella to be in another room, napping or engaging with a toy. “For individuals that have an important event on, we suggest that you find a safe and comfortable space for your pet,” says Fowler. “That may be in a room with some toys, treats and water to access where they can rest so you can focus on what you need to do. If you want to have your dog close to you, rewarding them with treats and attention, at times when they are displaying the behaviour you want to see.”

Advertisement

Related Article Pets Can having a dog boost your longevity? Here’s what science says If this is not possible – or you forget – there are ways to respond to unexpected canine interruptions. “Most of us have had moments where our dog decides to bark at the worst possible time,” says Dr Diana Barker, president of the Australian Veterinary Association. “The best approach is to stay as calm as you can.” “Barking is a form of communication, so it’s worth considering why your dog is barking in the first place. Are they alerting you to something, seeking attention, anxious or simply excited? If you can’t address it immediately, calmly moving them to another room with something appropriate to occupy them is generally more effective than reacting in frustration.” Like humans, dogs respond best to praise. iStock

Advertisement

And if you have raised your voice, there are ways to make amends. “Sadly, we can’t just apologise to our dogs but there are things we can do to help them decompress following stressful events,” says Jennings. “Decompression walks are my favourite, as allowing and even encouraging our dogs to put their big noses to work and use their superior sense of smell, is extremely relaxing, satisfying, enriching and also tiring for our dogs.” That, and a promise to be better prepared next time. Make the most of your health, relationships, fitness and nutrition with our Live Well newsletter. Get it in your inbox every Monday.

title: The high drama and budgie smugglers of ALP conferences past
text:
Advertisement

Political Sketch PoliticsFederalALP The high drama and budgie smugglers of ALP conferences past Tony Wright July 22, 2026 — 11:45am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

The days of Labor Party luminaries disporting themselves in budgie smugglers while surrounded by young bikini-clad women are, sadly for us in the political circus industry, long gone from ALP national conferences. So are endless nights when a prime minister disappeared into sweaty high-roller casino rooms to emerge boasting of fabulous luck at the blackjack table – which may or may not have been the case. Bob Hawke and Gough Whitlam have a beer poolside at the Florida Hotel, Terrigal, in February, 1975. Fairfax Archives Gone, too, are those times when two famously intimidating party arm-twisters enthroned in a smoke-filled interrogation cell skilfully terrorised colleagues into voting the right way. The Australian Labor Party, you probably haven’t noticed, is to hold its 50th national conference in Adelaide over the next three days.

Advertisement

It’ll be a stage-managed affair attended by thousands of delegates who will ritually disagree loudly with each other – AUKUS, Gaza and gas taxes come to mind – before voting as they are told by their various factional wranglers. It is, thus, largely a theatrical event designed to ensure Prime Minister Anthony Albanese emerges with the party’s blessing for whatever circumspect path he intends to take to the next election. Bob Hawke with Glenda Bowden, secretary to then-Treasurer and Deputy Prime Minister Jim Cairns at the ALP conference in Terrigal in 1975. Fairfax Archives It most certainly won’t come within whispering distance of the sort of wild theatricality of memorable ALP national conferences of the past. The most abandoned was held at the Florida Hotel in the NSW Central Coast town of Terrigal in February 1975.

Advertisement

It was so uninhibited that then-prime minister Gough Whitlam, a man of the classics, might have foreseen it as the beginning of his Fall of Rome, if he’d been thinking straight. Related Article Exclusive

ALP Three key policies up for debate at Labor’s national conference Party secretary Bob Hawke spent much of the conference around the pool in tight budgie smugglers. His skin became tanned in the Terrigal sun, and quivered with apparent pleasure as a scantily clad young woman laid a light flogging on him with a cat o’ nine tails at the nearby Old Sydney Town convict settlement mock-up. Back at the Florida, then-deputy prime minister Jim Cairns felt moved to announce to a reporter that he felt “a kind of love” for his principal private secretary, Junie Morosi. Australia was agog. Meanwhile, energy minister Rex Connor sat anxiously in his room, sweating on a call from a Pakistani “financier” Tirath Khemlani about a vast loan Khemlani was supposedly brokering for the Australian government.

Advertisement

The money never turned up, and the whole thing collapsed into one of the scandals that ruined the Whitlam government later in the year. Related Article Political leadership Albanese once took on his own frontbench. Now he’s in charge, that would never happen At Terrigal, it seemed the summer of love would never end. “We shall go from this conference with our program refreshed and, I believe, with our determination refreshed,” Whitlam declared. He had just returned from an extravagant 15-nation tour of mostly European cities, and hadn’t quite got out of the holiday mood. He wore a loud Hawaiian shirt as he delivered the keynote speech.

Advertisement

By the end of the year, Whitlam’s government was gone, dismissed by then-governor-general John Kerr and massively rejected by voters. When Hawke became Labor’s leader, he ensured the national conference was held at Hobart’s Wrest Point Casino. His days were spent listening to delegates passionately debating the party’s platform and devoted his nights to sessions at the blackjack table. He didn’t have to worry too much. In Wrest Point’s suite 1002, leaders of the dominant Right factions from NSW and Victoria, Graham “Richo” Richardson and fellow senator Robert Ray, sat by a well-stocked bar in a fug of Ray’s pipe smoke as a queue of delegates filed through; supplicants required to discuss how they should vote on the party’s platform at the following day’s formal sessions. The results, thus, were satisfactorily settled ahead of time. Numbers man Graham “Richo” Richardson stands guard at the door of Room 1002 in the Wrest Point casino in the 1980s. David Bartho

Advertisement

The 1998 conference was a victory dance – the leader of the Democrats, Cheryl Kernot, had defected to Labor, then in opposition under Kim Beazley. She was the star speaker at a gala dinner at a fancy Hobart venue up the Derwent River. When the text of her speech was leaked, some of the tough old guard refused to board the ferry to the dinner, muttering into their beers that this newcomer was up herself. The text read: “In a way I certainly didn’t expect it to, the earth moved on October the 15th. A fault line opened up at Kirribilli, and now it’s running all the way to the prime minister’s office in Canberra.” Cheryl Kernot with her new boss Kim Beazley after defecting from the Australian Democrats to join the Labor Party. It didn’t end well. Mike Bowers After Labor minders realised what the media would do with this extraordinary line, Kernot modified it slightly. “In a way I certainly didn’t expect it to, maybe the entire earth didn’t move, but it certainly seemed to on October 15,” she said.

Advertisement

Years later, it became known Kernot and Labor’s foreign affairs man, Gareth Evans, had been lovers when she defected. Perhaps the Earth had actually moved. Related Article Exclusive

Gambling Labor’s millions in gambling donations revealed amid last-ditch push for reform There have been a few lesser moments of theatre at national conferences in the years since. Mark Latham, then-opposition leader, was greeted at the 2004 conference by loudspeakers blasting the INXS rock song New Sensation. It turned out to be premature. In 2011, then-prime minister Julia Gillard praised from the stage former Labor prime ministers dating back to 1940. All but Kevin Rudd, who she’d de-throned the previous year.

Advertisement

Was she playing Lady Macbeth, some whispered? And here we are: 2026, where decidedly non-Labor Pauline Hanson is getting all the attention for performances elsewhere. Press gallery journalists – which is to say, those in the political circus business – will have to work hard to dredge drama out of Labor’s proceedings in Adelaide this week if Albanese has his way. He wouldn’t want a Terrible Terrigal on his hands. Cut through the noise of federal politics with news, views and expert analysis. Subscribers can sign up to our weekly Inside Politics newsletter.

title: Torrie Lewis downs five-time Olympic gold medallist in record-breaking win
text:
Advertisement

SportAthleticsCommonwealth Games Torrie Lewis downs five-time Olympic gold medallist in record-breaking win Roy Ward July 22, 2026 — 11:41am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Australia’s fastest woman just got faster, with sprinter Torrie Lewis smashing her own national 100m record and taking down a five-time Olympic champion in the process. Lewis, 21, won the 100m at the PTS meet in Banska Bystrica, Slovakia on Tuesday, posting a time of 11.04 seconds to beat Jamaican sprint great Elaine Thompson-Herah and Austria’s Christania Williams who both ran 11.06 seconds to finish second and third respectively. Australian sprinter Torrie Lewis has again broken her own national record. Getty Images The 34-year-old Thompson-Herah is one of the greatest women sprinters in history, a five-time Olympic gold medallist (with wins in the 100m, 200m and 4x100m relay at the Rio and Tokyo Games). She is not listed to compete in Glasgow. Queenslander Lewis first broke the national record as a 19-year-old in Canberra in 2024 when she ran 11.10 seconds, beating Melissa Breen’s record from 2014 of 11.11s. Lewis lowered that time again during heats at last year’s world athletics championships with a run of 11.08.

Advertisement

Her statement victory comes on the eve of the Commonwealth Games in Glasgow where Lewis will face stiff competition, but is showing she is in peak form and primed to push for gold. Lewis finished her night in Slovakia with another victory in the 200m, clocking 22.66s. Loading The results are a boost for Lewis, with the athletics competition at the 10-day Games starting on Monday. She had a mixed result at the world titles in Tokyo last September when she ran 11.14 in the semis to just miss out on becoming the first Australian woman to make a 100m final at that event.

Advertisement

Related Article Updated

World athletics championships Eight-centimetre hamstring tear ends Gout Gout’s under-20 world title tilt This latest record comes just months after Lewis was retrospectively awarded the gold medal in March for the 200m at the 2024 under-20 world championships after winner Adaejah Hodge had tested positive for a banned metabolic modulator. Hodge, who competes for the Virgin Islands, received a heavy discount to her ban which will allow her to compete in Glasgow, setting up a showdown with Lewis as Hodge has been named to race in the 100m and 200m. Lewis is scheduled to run in the 100m, 200m and 4x100m relay in Glasgow after being named in the 55-member Australian team along with fellow rising sprint stars Lachlan Kennedy (100m, 200m and 4x100m relay) and Eddie Nketia (100m). The competition is a valuable international building block on the path to the LA 2028 Olympic Games.

Advertisement

Related Article Commonwealth Games The 75-year-old great-grandmother making her Commonwealth Games debut Young sensation Gout Gout will not compete in Glasgow, having planned to run at this year’s world under-20 championships instead, but that was shelved after he suffered a serious hamstring injury in training. He is expected to be out of competition until next year. “The team is packed full of both proven world, Olympic and Paralympic champions and medallists and our next crop of junior talent,” Athletics Australia chief executive Simon Hollingsworth said of the Glasgow team. “There is amazing depth in the team. “There is a real history with Australian athletics and the Commonwealth Games, and the team wants to continue that history.

Advertisement

“We can’t wait to see what happens in Glasgow.” News, results and expert analysis from the weekend of sport are sent every Monday. Sign up for our Sport newsletter.

title: Struggling 10 News+ dumped from weekdays as Ten expands 5pm bulletins
text:
Advertisement

CultureTV & radioAustralian TV Struggling 10 News+ dumped from weekdays as Ten expands 5pm bulletins Michael Lallo July 22, 2026 — 11:35am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Network 10 is dumping its struggling nightly news and current affairs program 10 News+ from its weekday line-up, although the program will continue to air on Sundays. In its place, the broadcaster will expand its flagship 5pm weeknight news bulletins from one hour to 90 minutes. 10 News+, which launched as a one-hour format in June 2025 before being trimmed to 30 minutes in January, currently airs six nights a week, Sunday to Friday. The changes will take effect on August 10, and 10 News+ will retain its 6.30pm slot on Sundays. 10 News+ co-hosts Amelia Brace and Denham Hitchcock. The struggling show will now air on Sunday nights only. Janie Barrett The ambitious program, hosted by Amelia Brace and Denham Hitchcock, replaced nightly panel show The Project, which drew larger audiences but became unsustainable due to high production costs. Year-to-date ratings figures for 10 News+ show its perilous state, with the Monday to Friday edition averaging about 151,000 national viewers, while its Sunday edition drew 160,000. The Project, meanwhile, attracted between 238,000 and 357,000 viewers nationally the week before it was axed. Ten’s 5pm bulletins have fared better than 10 News+, averaging more than 300,000 viewers this year.

Advertisement

Related Article Analysis

Today Today may have rid itself of Karl Stefanovic, but his departure exposes another problem Staff were informed of the changes on Wednesday morning by Martin White, Ten’s vice-president of news. In a note to his team, White said, “While we are still finalising the format, we are making this change because our audience has told us they want more local news. We will still give audiences investigations, exclusives and insight via the 10 News+ team across the week, as well as on Sunday’s dedicated program. This will provide more content for the states and lighten the workload on teams around the country. “I want to be clear – we are incredibly proud of all our news bulletins, and the quality journalism we produce daily. Last month, 10 News saw its biggest monthly national total audience since January. Across the year, 10 News’ streaming audience is up 45% on 2025. 10’s Late News is [now attracting] its biggest audiences since its return in 2024. This is a necessary pivot to maximise our resourcing and deliver more local news for our audiences.” The crew for the final episode of The Project on June 26, 2025: Sarah Harris, Carrie Bickmore, Sam Taunton, Dave Hughes, Georgie Tunny and Waleed Aly.

Advertisement

Contrary to recent rumours, Ten confirmed it would not outsource its news production to a rival network, nor would it initiate large-scale job cuts. Related Article Review What to stream this week: A hot new Aussie medical drama, plus five more picks Fewer than five staff in back-end roles are expected to be made redundant, while the journalists and producers working on 10 News+ will be redeployed to Ten’s early evening bulletins. Ten’s 90-minute weeknight bulletins will feature longer-form reporting, with current 10 News+ co-anchor Hitchcock also working on several documentaries. Brace will soon take maternity leave but will remain with Ten’s news division. Ten will continue broadcasting local weeknight bulletins in each major city, while maintaining national weekend bulletins. (Perth is now an exception, with Narelda Jacobs presenting a local weekend bulletin in her home state.)

Advertisement

title: The 75-year-old great-grandmother making her Commonwealth Games debut
text:
Advertisement

SportCommonwealth Games The 75-year-old great-grandmother making her Commonwealth Games debut Hannah Kennelly July 22, 2026 — 11:30am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Jacky Hudson and her family are familiar with sport on the biggest stage. Her grandson, Zac Laybutt, plays in the NRL with the North Queensland Cowboys. Her son, Stephen Laybutt, represented Australia as an Olyroo at the Sydney Olympics. Jacky Hudson will represent Australia in lawn bowls at the Commonwealth Games. Bowls Australia Photography, Paul A. Broben Now, at 75, it’s Hudson’s turn. Later this month, Hudson – a grandmother of five and a great-grandmother of two – will have her chance in the sporting spotlight when she steps out in the green and gold at the Commonwealth Games in Glasgow, as part of the lawn bowls squad.

Advertisement

“I’m probably still in shock a little but absolutely delighted and really proud that all the hard work has paid off,” she told this masthead. “It’s a privilege as well. I’m looking at it as a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity unless I still want to hang around until the next Comm Games or world championships,” she laughed. Born in Papua New Guinea, Hudson – who is vision-impaired – admitted she was “no good at sport” growing up and never saw anything athletic as a feasible pathway for herself. She moved to a boarding school in Sydney and was involved in community activities but never as an athlete. Hudson on the green. Bowls Australia Photography, Paul A. Broben Five decades later, Hudson and her husband Rob moved to Port Stephens in New South Wales. Rob started playing bowls socially and invited Hudson to try as well.

Advertisement

What began as a casual hobby quickly snowballed and Hudson started to play competitively with other vision-impaired athletes, before earning a place in Bowls Australia’s high-performance squad four years ago. In 2023, Hudson and her teammate Jake Fehlberg won gold at the 2023 World Bowls Championships. While Hudson is one of Australia’s oldest Commonwealth Games debutants this year, the nations’ lawn bowls team – known as the Jackaroos – is surprisingly youthful. The average age of the bowls team is actually younger than the Australian cricket team – a fact that makes Hudson laugh. “People think it [lawn bowls] is all old people, retirees and they’re in long white clothes, but these young ones make a real living out of the game,” she said. “There’s good prizemoney and you get to travel, I don’t think a lot of people realise that.”

Advertisement

Countering misconceptions and stereotypes around lawn bowls is something Dawn Hayman is very familiar with. The 29-year-old is one of the youngest members on the Jackaroos squad and boasts an impressive resume, winning the 2025 trans-Tasman women’s championship and claiming gold in the 2023 world bowls championships. Australian Lawn Bowls Champion Dawn Hayman. Bowls Australia Photography, Paul A. Broben Hayman was first introduced to lawn bowls as a nine-year-old at Fairy Meadows Bowls Club in NSW, although she jokes she wasn’t immediately enamoured with the sport. “My parents wanted me to try bowls and I said, ‘No way I’m never gonna play that game because it’s for old people’,” she laughed.

Advertisement

“They eventually got me out there and I haven’t stopped since.” Both Hayman and Hudson agree lawn bowls’ inclusivity is what makes it such an attractive and rewarding sport to play. “The sport is so diverse, you can play with a disability in a wheelchair, whether you’re male or female, it doesn’t matter,” Hayman said. As a vision-impaired athlete, Hudson sometimes struggles to see down the end of the green during lawn bowls competition. Depending on where she’s playing, she will have a director to help her understand how far she needs to throw. Despite her success and accolades, Hudson remains humble, preferring to focus on the achievements of her children and grandchildren.

Advertisement

Related Article Commonwealth Games Why McKeown’s Comm Games withdrawal opens door for O’Callaghan to chase history “People always ask where my medals are, but I tend to put them away in a box,” she joked. But she said she would display a Commonwealth Games gold medal at home. “That’s what we all aim for, and not everybody’s going to get one ... all you can do is put in your best effort, and hope for the hope for the results. “As long as you know you’ve done your best, that’s all you can do.”

Advertisement

title: 'Strong concerns': Australia meets with China following ballistic missile test
text:
Australia has raised its concerns with China over its nuclear-capable missile test in the South Pacific, noting many of the region's nations also shared the same view.

Foreign Minister Penny Wong met with her Chinese counterpart, Wang Yi, on the sidelines of ASEAN talks in the Philippines capital, Manila, on Tuesday evening.

"You speak about the importance of mutual understanding. I agree, and I expect we will discuss our concerns ... including around peace and stability in our region, safe and professional military conduct, and the importance of transparency and reassurance of intent," Wong said in opening remarks.

Wong noted the "strong concerns" expressed by many Pacific Island countries about the submarine-launched missile earlier in July.

The two foreign ministers also discussed the importance of peace and stability in the Indo-Pacific.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

"You've often heard me say that we will cooperate where we can and disagree where we must, and we want to continue to engage because we believe that is in our national interest," Wong said.

Australia was only given a few hours' notice by China of the test, which was launched within hours of Prime Minister Anthony Albanese signing a new defence alliance with Fiji.

The missile is understood to have landed near Tuvalu.

Albanese slammed the launch as a "provocative act" while other Pacific nations including New Zealand, Tuvalu and Fiji condemned the incident.

Solomon Islands Prime Minister Matthew Wale was standing beside his Australian counterpart in his country's capital Honiara in early July when he said the test was "not the act of a friend".

Albanese has previously flagged a strong statement would be issued by the Pacific Islands Forum, but the group's disagreement on the wording of the text has resulted in a delay.

A foreign ministers' meeting of the forum will be held in August, where the issue is likely to be discussed.

title: 'Business-friendly socialism': What are new UK Prime Minister Andy Burnham's policies?
text:
In brief Andy Burnham was the mayor of Greater Manchester before replacing Keir Starmer as the United Kingdom's prime minister.

Burnham says he'll lead a "cost of living government", with one of his first policies cutting taxes on electricity bills.

Andy Burnham, sworn in this week as the United Kingdom's seventh prime minister in a decade, has opened his tenure with a slew of new policies, branding his own political ideology as "business-friendly socialism".

Burnham was sworn in as the UK's new prime minister on Monday, replacing Keir Starmer, who resigned following months of pressure from his own MPs after just over two years in the job.

The former Greater Manchester mayor, nicknamed the 'King of the North' for his defence of the region during his nine-year stint as mayor, used his first days in office to signal a new leftward shift for the Labour government.

On his first full day, Burnham told the inaugural meeting of his cabinet that he would lead a "cost of living government".

By the second day, his first concrete policies had arrived.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

What are Andy Burnham's policies?

Burnham has branded his political ideology "business-friendly socialism" — with a distinct focus on cost of living measures.

He has unveiled some early policies designed "to do some things that people will feel and feel quite quickly", especially those on the lowest incomes — adding that a longer-term plan would be set out later in the year.

On Tuesday, Burnham announced he will cut taxes on electricity bills — one of the main drivers of the UK's cost of living crisis — in his first move to ease pressures that have angered the public and helped stoke political instability.

Burnham said his government would remove the value-added tax from domestic electricity bills from 1 October, cutting about £45 ($86) from the average annual bill of about £1,860 ($3,554) that households pay.

"We're taking immediate action to cut taxes on energy bills, put more money in people's pockets and bring back hope," Burnham said in a statement, having pledged to give people more "breathing space" in their lives.

The government said it would be funded by cancelling Starmer's plan for a digital ID scheme that would have cost the taxpayer £1.8 billion ($3.44 billion) — but former minister Darren Jones said the ID program had been "unfunded".

The energy announcement is designed to show that Burnham will move quickly to improve living standards after years of stagnation.

He said on Monday his early policies might not "take all of the pressure off" people struggling with rising prices, and that his 10-year plan will be unveiled later this year.

What else is on the agenda?

During his first speech as prime minister on Monday, Burnham also said his first ambition was to end rough sleeping in the UK.

He is reportedly considering rent control measures, alongside plans to build more public housing to help bring down welfare spending.

Burnham has pledged to re-industrialise the country and push through greater public control of utilities.

He is also expected to look at possible caps on bus fares — an echo of his policy in Manchester, where riders would not pay more than £2 ($3.80) — and other measures to back up his assertion that he would "put the care of people at the heart of everything I do".

He has long supported the idea that social care should be more of a universal system, but says he wants to see a "fair and lasting" reduction to welfare spending — through improving employment support rather than cutting payment benefits.

On immigration, Burnham recently said net migration "needs to fall further", though he hasn't set a specific target.

He also intends to decentralise power away from London to other cities and regions, promising the biggest-ever "rebalancing of power".

As part of this, he will spend "at least one day a week" working in Manchester at 'No. 10 North', and will move some government operations from London to Manchester.

He also said last September that he would like to see the UK back in the European Union within his lifetime, but said he does not want to re-run a Brexit referendum at the moment.

— With additional reporting by Reuters, Agence France-Presse, and the Australian Associated Press.

title: Flights to return after 40-year hiatus as Trump promises to help Lebanon 'a lot'
text:
In brief Joseph Aoun is the first Lebanese president invited to Washington since 2009.

Aoun asked US President Donald Trump to continue backing the Lebanese army, along with support for an Israeli withdrawal.

United States President Donald Trump vowed on Wednesday to help Lebanon "a lot" as its president, Joseph Aoun, sought the US' backing for a fragile effort to disarm Hezbollah and forge a path to peace with Israel.

Meeting Aoun at the White House, Trump said Lebanon had been "a very mistreated country" that had been "hit hard" for decades.

"It's probably a dangerous place in a lot of ways," Trump told reporters. "But they love the country ... and we're going to help it. We're going to help it a lot."

Aoun, the first Lebanese president invited to Washington since 2009, asked Trump for political support and continued backing for the Lebanese army, which has begun deploying in southern areas under a US-backed framework agreement with Israel.

"We need to support the LAF," Aoun said, referring to the Lebanese Armed Forces. "Without the LAF, everything will collapse."

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

Aoun's nearly two-hour visit took place as Israeli troops continue to occupy a swath of the country's south and hundreds of thousands of Lebanese remain displaced following Israeli strikes. Hezbollah has rejected the government's direct talks with Israel as well as efforts by the state to disarm it.

The talks highlighted Aoun's delicate balancing act — seeking Trump's help to secure an Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon while simultaneously facing pressure from the US and Israel to disarm Hezbollah, which remains a formidable fighting force despite recent setbacks.

The Lebanese president, a former army commander, said the military was "the backbone of security and stability" and the country's most trusted institution.

In a statement after the talks, the Lebanese presidency said Aoun stressed to Trump the "urgent need for a complete Israeli withdrawal from Lebanese territory," calling it "an essential step to consolidate stability and enable the Lebanese state to extend its full sovereignty exclusively through its own forces."

Flights resume

Following his meeting with Aoun, Trump said he would direct his administration to allow all US airline carriers to fly directly to Lebanon— a historic policy shift more than 40 years in the making.

US flights were suspended in 1985 after the hijacking of TWA Flight 847 from Athens, Greece, en route to Rome, Italy, that was forced to land in Beirut. During the 17-day ordeal, a US Navy diver was brutally killed.

The Federal Aviation Administration and Transportation Department did not immediately comment.

Trump's visit came as the Lebanese army moved into an area of the south under a deal with Israel aimed at proving Lebanon can extend state authority and disarm Iran-backed Hezbollah.

But in a sign of the fragility of the agreement, the Lebanese army said Israeli troops fired near its soldiers deploying in a so-called "pilot zone" Israel is supposed to withdraw from.

Asked whether the US would put additional pressure on Israel to withdraw, Trump said: "We'll be looking at that."

Lebanon is under pressure to prove that it can and will disarm Hezbollah, which Israel has set as a precondition for ending its occupation in southern Lebanon.

The "pilot zones" where the Lebanese army is deploying are primarily located just outside territory Israeli forces invaded after Hezbollah opened rocket fire on Israel and drew Lebanon into the Middle East war in March.

The army said on Tuesday it had begun deploying to one of three pilot zones, the village of Zawtar al-Gharbiya. Israel's military said it would be "adjusting its force posture" in one pilot area.

Hours later, however, the Lebanese army said Israeli forces "opened fire in the vicinity of these units," adding that the incident would hinder the deployment.

The Israel Defense Forces (IDF) said it had only fired "warning shots into the air" after Lebanese soldiers entered an area that was "not part of the pilot area".

"The Lebanese Armed Forces personnel withdrew from the area, and no injuries were reported," it added.

'Terrorist infrastructure'

Aoun has staunchly criticised Hezbollah for forcing Lebanon into the war and has vowed since taking office in early 2025 to bring all weapons under state control.

But Hezbollah has repeatedly rejected laying down its arms, and the group has flatly opposed both the deal and the Israel-Lebanon negotiations that underpin it.

Aoun said the framework agreement did not require parliamentary endorsement and was a presidential prerogative under Lebanon's constitution.

US secretary of state Marco Rubio said after meeting Aoun on Sunday that Lebanon would "never truly get peace" as long as Hezbollah remained armed.

Rubio also praised what he called the Lebanese government's effort to reclaim sovereignty, disarm Hezbollah and dismantle its "terrorist infrastructure."

Aoun's push comes after Israeli strikes in the latest conflict killed more than 4,300 people in Lebanon, according to Lebanese authorities.

The violence has dropped off sharply since the signing of a framework agreement between the United States and Iran last month and the Lebanon-Israel deal.

Trump also used the meeting to warn Yemen's Iran-backed Houthi rebels against following through on threats to blockade Saudi ports.

"If something like that happens, we take care of it," Trump told reporters. "We've done that with the Houthis before, and we haven't heard from them in a while since we did what we did originally."

title: Bigger, richer and messier: The World Cup that reshaped FIFA's showpiece
text:
In brief Cape Verde's run helped justify FIFA's expanded 48-team format.

The tournament delivered record crowds and revenue, but controversy followed FIFA.

The biggest World Cup in history came to a close on Monday, with Spain defeating Argentina to lift football's biggest prize.

On many measures, the tournament was a resounding success. But it was also a World Cup of contradictions.

Cape Verde's remarkable run became the tournament's fairy-tale story, vindicating FIFA's decision to expand the format to 48 teams.

Millions of fans flocked to the United States, Canada and Mexico, packing out sold-out stadiums with the hosts receiving near-universal praise for their hospitality.

Norwegian fans took over the New York subway for the iconic "Norwegian Row", while Scotland's Tartan Army drank Boston's bars dry, becoming among the tournament's most memorable fan stories.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

But the World Cup was also marked by controversy.

US President Donald Trump's intervention over a one-match suspension handed to USA striker Folarin Balogun drew widespread criticism.

US President Donald Trump (left) personally asked FIFA president Gianni Infantino (right) to review a one-game suspension handed to a USA player. Source: Bloomberg / Annabelle Gordon/ via Getty Images

The treatment of the Iranian team and Somali referee Omar Artan, as well as increased prices and debate over favouritism, also fuelled criticism of FIFA.

So what defined this World Cup? And are FIFA's biggest changes here to stay?

FIFA's most lucrative World Cup

FIFA was undoubtedly the biggest winner.

The president of footballs governing body, Gianni Infantino, on Saturday declared it "the greatest FIFA World Cup of all time", surpassing all expectations.

And he had good reason to be happy.

While FIFA is yet to release its official financial results, The Guardian reported the tournament generated a record $21.4 billion in revenue — far exceeding pre-tournament forecasts.

Listen To Podcast · English · Sports World Cup Daily | The 90+ Podcast World Cup 2026 wrap: Highlights, controversies and the future 21/07/2026 14:37

Spain beats Lionel Messi's Argentina to win 2026 World Cup 20/07/2026 14:03

England defeats France to claim bronze, Mbappé leads World Cup goal record 19/07/2026 10:08

Will FIFA keep hydration breaks at the next World Cup? 17/07/2026 16:17

Heartbreak for England as Argentina heads to World Cup final against Spain 16/07/2026 12:12 View more

However, that success was often at the expense of fans.

Those who made the trip needed deep pockets, with ticket prices skyrocketing with the introduction of dynamic pricing and an inflated resale market that saw FIFA take a 30 per cent cut.

Concerns high prices would result in empty seats proved largely unfounded.

FIFA said more than 6.6 million spectators attended matches up to and including the semi-finals, with stadiums reaching 99.7 per cent capacity — the highest attendance rate in World Cup history.

Viewers at home were also subject to more advertising after the introduction of controversial hydration breaks.

But that did not deter viewers, with FIFA claiming 5.2 billion people — nearly two-thirds of the world's population — engaged with the World Cup at least once.

University of Sydney sports studies expert Dr Steve Georgakis said he did not expect future tournaments to be any less expensive.

"One of the biggest negative aspects of it is that it's really taking the sport away from the working classes, the people whose communities are besotted by soccer," he told SBS News.

Expanded format

The biggest structural change was the expansion from 32 teams to 48.

Critics argued the larger tournament — there were 104 matches compared with 64 in recent tournaments — would reduce the quality of the competition and produce more lop-sided matches.

Instead, World Cup debutantes such as minnows Curaçao, Cape Verde, Uzbekistan and Jordan proved them wrong.

Aside from Curaçao's 7-1 defeat to Germany, none of the debutantes suffered heavy losses and each of them scored at least one goal.

Cape Verde's World Cup campaign became one of the highlights of the tournament. Source: AAP / Fotoarena/ Sipa USA

Cape Verde, with a population of about 500,000, defied all expectations, holding eventual champions Spain to a scoreless draw in their opening game.

The island nation qualified for the Round of 32, where they took runner-ups Argentina to extra time before losing 3-2 in what was one of the most exciting matches of the tournament.

Former FIFA referee Christina Unkel said she thought the expanded format was a boon for smaller countries, which benefited from playing in large tournaments.

"The new teams to the tournament showed that they're there to play ball," she told SBS News.

"When you open up opportunities and slots for teams to make the World Cup, there is more funding that happens in those federations to be competitive.

"You continue to grow and increase the game because they say, 'Hey, I actually have an opportunity', and it's not just the powerhouse football clubs."

Georgakis agreed the expansion had been vindicated.

"In all of the group stage matches, every game except for a couple, right to the death, the teams were playing for something," he told SBS News.

"There were no real weak teams. International soccer has come to the point now where the smaller nations really trouble the very big nations, and we saw that."

FIFA has discussed expanding the format to 64 teams for the 2030 World Cup, though the proposal is divisive.

The controversies

However there were plenty of scandals.

The biggest centred on Trump's intervention over Balogun's suspension.

The suspension of Folarin Balogun's one-match ban following his red card against Bosnia and Herzegovina rocked the footballing world. Source: AAP / Benjamin Fanjoy / EPA

The USA striker had been due to miss the Round of 16 against Belgium after receiving a red card against Bosnia and Herzegovina, resulting in a one-match suspension.

But the one-match ban was inexplicably suspended for a year by FIFA, allowing Balogun to play. Trump later confirmed he had personally asked Infantino to review the case.

Infantino defended the decision, saying it was made by an independent judicial panel and his conversation with Trump had not influenced the outcome.

But the decision received widespread condemnation from former players, journalists and other footballing bodies.

Some experts warned it could set a "dangerous precedent" as political leaders sought to influence football's governing body.

"That's a real sour point," Georgakis said.

"It's very clear there that Donald Trump had a very unsettling relationship with FIFA."

Another controversy emerged before the tournament had even started.

Despite holding valid travel documents and FIFA accreditation, Somali referee Omar Artan was denied entry to the United States on arrival in Miami and missed the tournament.

Iran also accused organisers of unfair treatment after travel restrictions forced their players to fly between their base camp in Mexico and the United States for each match.

Argentina, meanwhile, faced accusations of favourable refereeing after their 3-2 Round of 16 victory over Egypt.

Egypt filed a complaint against FIFA after the match, arguing that an early goal was incorrectly ruled out for a foul in the build-up and saying they should have been awarded a penalty in a separate play.

Egypt's head coach Hossam Hassan was highly critical of the officiating in his side's loss to Argentina. Source: Getty / Buda Mendes

But Unkel said the incidents were different from an officiating perspective and warned against interpreting difficult refereeing decisions as evidence of bias.

"The Egypt-Argentina was what I would call as a perfect storm," she said.

"It's on the heels of sentiments that people had even before the tournament started that Argentina is historically benefiting from these decisions and these calls.

"You're going into that ... even as a referee, knowing that, everyone's looking for something.

"The nuance of officiating subjective decisions is incredibly important to understand, and it's very easy to paint broad brush strokes versus looking at the details."

Georgakis said controversy was almost inevitable at an event as large as the World Cup.

"A World Cup without controversies is not a World Cup," he said.

But he said he would ultimately remember it as a success.

"I'll remember it for one thing, and that is the fact that we had 48 teams taking part in 104 games," he said.

"The World Cup has been ramped up, and you can argue that it is now truly an inclusive World Cup."
