title: Two years into the government's social housing push, a new report says it's well behind target
text:
In brief A new report has suggested that the government is well behind its social housing targets.

The government is now two years into a five-year target period.

Less than four per cent of the social homes promised by the Albanese government's signature social housing fund have been delivered, two years into a five-year target period.

As of May 2026, 1432 of the Housing Australia Future Fund's target of 40,000 new social and affordable homes had been delivered, the Australian National Audit Office found in a report released on Tuesday.

The $10 billion fund's forecast to hit its target by mid-2029 faced "considerable uncertainty" as of June, the auditor-general found.

It's another setback for the government's broader housing supply agenda, with the construction industry about a year behind on its target to deliver 1.2 million new homes in the five years to June 2029.

Meanwhile, the social housing waitlist has grown from 155,000 to 169,000 in the 10 years to June 2024.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

While Treasury was largely effective in designing the fund, the department had failed to take clear responsibility for delivering the program and provided little transparency about how it was progressing, the audit found.

Although funding applications opened in January 2024, Treasury had not finalised delivery arrangements until May 2026.

"The delayed finalisation of most governance and oversight arrangements has limited the ability of Treasury to systematically monitor and manage program risks and performance over time," the auditor-general said.

"Once finalised, Treasury has not always implemented governance and oversight arrangements as intended, with sometimes out-of-date and inconsistent arrangements.

"Risk management arrangements are insufficient, including for shared risks."

The fund works by giving money to providers to either build new homes or purchase newly constructed homes from developers.

Once built, homes are subsidised by the government to provide tenants below-market rates for 25 years, at a cost of about $393,000 per dwelling in today's money.

Of the 1432 homes delivered by the fund, 762 were new homes constructed by housing providers, and 670 were newly built by private developers and purchased by a housing provider before, during or immediately after development.

The audit made five recommendations to improve governance, risk management, performance review and transparency of the program.

Housing Minister Clare O'Neil welcomed the report findings and accepted all recommendations.

"Importantly, the report found Treasury's design of the Housing Australia Future Fund was largely effective and supported by largely sound policy advice," she said.

"This program is being delivered in one of the toughest construction markets in decades.

"As building and financing costs increased, Treasury identified those pressures early, and the government acted to protect our commitment to deliver more social and affordable homes."

Opposition housing spokesman Andrew Bragg said the report laid bare how the fund failed to deliver.

"Only a handful of houses have been built, and just under half are not new," he said.

"Buying homes is not building homes. It does nothing to fix Australia's housing supply crisis."

title: 'Kings of the World': Spain's World Cup stars get a hero's welcome from fans and royals
text:
In Brief Spain's players presented the World Cup winner's trophy to the Spanish Royal Family.

Some players, like forward Borja Iglesias, played music on a DJ set on top of the parade bus.

New World Cup champions Spain enjoyed a vibrant celebration parade on an open-top bus through the streets of Madrid on Monday evening, with a jubilant crowd of an estimated two million people out to celebrate, according to local authorities.

The players, some shirtless, others holding drinks, set off on their procession under a blazing sun, along the main arteries in the city centre where a sea of fans in red and yellow were waiting, before arriving at Cibeles square where they took to the stage and partied into the night.

The squad and coach Luis de la Fuente were introduced one by one to the fans, each with their own choice of music that they sung along to, wearing "We are champions" shirts.

"(Spain) showed the world what it is to work together, with one mind and one heart," said pop superstar Shakira in a video message played to the team and crowd.

De la Fuente offered a similar message, hailing his players as the "best in the world" and telling fans not to forget that "together we are stronger".

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

After flying back from the United States to Madrid, the players were received by King Felipe VI and the royal family earlier Monday, and then Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez Pérez-Castejón, before kicking off the parade.

Spain's players paraded onboard an open-top bus through the streets of Madrid. Source: AFP / Cesar Manso

Players passed around the gleaming World Cup trophy and waved to their adoring supporters, some lining balconies, others on top of bus stops, the day after defeating Argentina 1-0 in extra-time in New Jersey.

Ferran Torres's strike in extra-time was enough to bring Spain their second World Cup, following a first victory in 2010.

Authorities estimated that around 120,000 fans were gathered at Cibeles square alone, where the celebrations reached their climax. Source: AFP / Pierre-Philippe Marcou

"You have shown your grit, your resilience and your courage ... combining head and heart," the king told La Roja's stars, who were dressed in suits but changed to more casual clothing for their parade on a hot summer's day.

"Kings of the world" ran the words on the front of their vehicle, with fans chanting "champions" to their heroes.

Spain captain Rodri held the World Cup trophy aloft at the top of the stairs as he descended from the Iberia plane that flew the team home after clinching their second star.

Later at Moncloa, Manchester City midfielder Rodri said this was the crowning achievement of his career.

"I've always said it, winning with your country is the most beautiful thing there is, above all a World Cup," said Rodri.

"This generation grew up watching (Iker) Casillas and (Andres) Iniesta lift the cup and that we can do it today is the biggest thing you can do in football."

Many younger supporters were not yet born when Iniesta's extra-time goal against the Netherlands secured Spain's first World Cup in South Africa 16 years ago.

"I'm really happy about the second star" Spain has earned, "very joyful," said Hector Mollar, a 14-year-old student, who said he was able to see Torres, Lamine Yamal and Mikel Merino as the bus parade passed.

It is "very different" to live a day like today than to have someone tell you about it, Hector added.

Match-winner Torres joked he was "flying, literally" after the team returned from the United States after beating Argentina in New Jersey.

title: Legendary media figure Derryn Hinch makes his final headlines with state funeral
text:
in brief Derryn Hinch was honoured with a state funeral in Melbourne on Monday.

Mourners gathered to express their condolences and share stories about the bombastic figure.

Derryn Hinch has been remembered for making waves as one of Australia's most outspoken and influential voices.

Longtime friend and colleague Darren James led mourners through a state funeral for the former broadcaster and senator on Monday.

"He was a journalist, a newspaper reporter, a TV presenter, a godfather, an author, a senator and, a prisoner ... he did it all," James told the service.

"Some people pass through life without leaving a ripple, he created tsunamis."

Hinch died on 10 July at the age of 82 after a lengthy health struggle, much of which he documented for followers on social media.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

The service for the lifelong atheist, who went to jail for identifying a paedophile Catholic priest before his trial, was held at the Timber Yard events space in Port Melbourne, near factories and apartments devoid of any religious leaning.

It attracted notable figures, including news presenters Ray Martin and Denis Walter, entertainers Rhonda Burchmore and Greg Evans, and former Crown executive Ann Peacock.

Other mourners, including Labor MP Danny Pearson, who attended on behalf of Premier Jacinta Allan, streamed in to pay their respects.

"Not all of us knew him personally, but for many it felt like we did," Pearson said.

An Australian flag was draped over his casket, surrounded by vases of red roses symbolising deep love, respect, courage and enduring affection.

Victorian MP Danny Pearson spoke at Derryn Hinch's state funeral, appearing on behalf of Premier Jacinta Allan. Source: AAP / Eddie Jim

Hinch initially moved from New Zealand to Australia to pursue a career in journalism, climbing the ranks to become editor of The Sun before turning to a career in radio.

He became known as the "human headline" — a moniker he enthusiastically adopted — for his outspoken and often controversial opinions.

Former 3AW colleagues Denis O'Kane and Tony Tardio spoke of their love for Hinch, who was well known for his hatred of child sex offenders and those who shielded them.

"There will never be another like him, even his name was unique," Tardio said.

"He was a journalistic James Bond, drama followed him everywhere — even to his first wedding. And then he went on to marry three other times."

His ex-wife and friend, actor Jacki Weaver, sent a video tribute from America describing Hinch as a wonderful stepfather and companion.

She also referenced Hinch's reputation as a notorious ladies' man.

Derryn Hinch worked across the Australian media landscape, and many of his former colleagues' spoke of their love for the broadcaster during his service. Source: AAP / Christopher Hopkins

"I'd like to pay my respects to all the women who loved Derryn, and who loved him back," she said.

"I won't name them all ... we don't have time."

While commanding the airwaves for decades as a talkback radio host on Melbourne radio station 3AW, Hinch became known for his uninhibited takes and a tendency to court controversy.

Later, Hinch turned to the political arena, forming Derryn Hinch's Justice Party and serving as a senator for Victoria in the federal parliament from 2016 to 2019.

His party focused on reforms to the justice system, including better protections for children, tightening of bail and parole laws, and the establishment of a sex offenders' register.

Hinch's final farewell ended with a eulogy by fellow media stalwart Martin, who said his mate would have loved the idea of having a state funeral and a standing ovation.

The service was brought to a close with the music of That's Life by Frank Sinatra, often used as the sign-off from his daily radio show.

title: Changing of the guard: The young stars taking over from Messi, Ronaldo, and Neymar
text:
Key Points Spain's 2026 World Cup win closed the book on football's golden generation, with Messi, Ronaldo and Neymar, all bowing out empty-handed.

A new wave of young stars is now poised to inherit the game.

When Spanish winger Ferran Torres swept the ball past Argentine goalkeeper Emiliano Martínez in the 106th minute at New York New Jersey Stadium on Monday morning, he did more than win Spain a second World Cup title. He drew the curtain on an era.

Lionel Messi, 39, wept after the match; beaten in a final for the first time and denied the chance to claim the tournament's Golden Boot for top goal-scorer from France's Kylian Mbappé.

Cristiano Ronaldo's Portugal had long since gone home, the 41-year-old's World Cup days now behind him. Neymar, 34, patched together for one last Brazilian campaign, could not drag his side past the Round of 16.

Croatia's Luka Modrić, 40, and veteran goalkeepers Manuel Neuer, 40, (Germany) and Guillermo Ochoa, 41 (Mexico) were among other members of a 'golden generation' that took its collective bow in North America, and almost every one of them left empty-handed.

For all its nostalgia, the 2026 tournament was equally an audition. So, who inherits the game now that these legends have moved on?

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

Here are the names to build the next decade around.

Lamine Yamal (Spain)

He came within a fingertip of settling the final, a curling stoppage-time free-kick drawing the save of the tournament from Martínez.

Lamine Yamal, 19, was the creative heartbeat of Spain's 2026 World Cup triumph. Source: AAP / Alexandre Martins/DPPI/IPA Sport 2/ipa-agency.net//Sipa USA

Across all seven games he was the creative heartbeat of the world champions, drifting in from the right flank to pull defences apart and set the tempo of Spain's attack.

Barcelona's number 10 already had a domestic treble and a European Championship to his name. Now, at just 19, he adds a world title — a haul most players never assemble across an entire career, let alone before turning 20.

Pau Cubarsí (Spain)

Arguably Spain's most important player, the 19-year-old centre-back anchored a defence that conceded just once all tournament. His six clean sheets in seven matches made him the fastest to that mark in World Cup history, past a record Italy's Paolo Maldini set in 1990.

Pau Cubarsí, 19, anchored a Spain defence that conceded just once en route to the 2026 world title. Source: AAP / Cody Grubbs/Sipa USA

He shackled Mbappé for 90 minutes in the semi-final, yet it's his passing that sets him apart: no player sent more balls into the opposition half.

Cubarsí's performances earned him the FIFA Young Player Award at the tournament.

A LaLiga regular for Barcelona since before he turned 17, he reads danger a decade ahead of his years. Sky Sports UK pundits are already tipping him to become a future top-five centre-back of all time.

Gilberto Mora (Mexico)

The youngest player at the entire tournament at 17, he grew into it superbly, running the midfield against Ecuador in the Round of 32 as the youngest man to start a knockout tie since Pelé — orchestrating a composed 2-0 win.

Gilberto Mora, 17, was the youngest player at the 2026 World Cup and starred in Mexico's midfield. Source: AAP / Ismael Rosas/ Eyepix Group/Sipa USA

The Club Tijuana playmaker, who operates as a left-footed attacking midfielder, is already the youngest goalscorer in Liga MX history and arrived having become the youngest player ever to win a senior international trophy — the 2025 Gold Cup, at 16.

Real Madrid, PSG, Chelsea, and Barcelona have all been linked; with a release clause reported around €20m ($32m), a move to Europe feels a matter of when, not if.

Yan Diomande (Côte d'Ivoire)

The breakout player of the group stage, he set up Nicolas Pépé's opener against Curaçao to send Côte d'Ivoire into the knockouts for the first time ever, and became the only player this century to complete 10 dribbles and create 10 chances across his first three World Cup games.

Yan Diomande, 19, was one of the breakout stars of the 2026 World Cup, helping Côte d'Ivoire reach the knockouts for the first time. Source: AAP / Baptiste Fernandez/Icon Sport/Sipa USA

All raw pace and fearless dribbling, the 19-year-old winger plays in Germany for RB Leipzig, where he was named Bundesliga Rookie of the Season after 12 goals and eight assists — form he carried in from a standout run at the Africa Cup of Nations earlier this year.

Liverpool and Barcelona are circling a player now valued past €100 million ($163 million).

Lucas Herrington (Australia)

The tournament's most unlikely riser. The 18-year-old centre-back made his World Cup debut in the final group game against Paraguay and put in a composed 90-minute shift that helped Australia to a clean sheet and into the Round of 32. In doing so, he became the youngest Socceroo ever to start a World Cup match and the youngest player in the US domestic league, the MLS, to feature at a World Cup.

Lucas Herrington, 18, became the youngest Socceroo to play at a World Cup. Source: AAP / LVDIDI/SPP/Sipa USA

Barely known outside the A-League a year ago, a January move to the Colorado Rapids changed everything: he walked straight into the starting eleven and has played every minute since.

At 6'4" and unflappable, he's already had a bid by Barcelona rejected by his club, and Liverpool are reportedly scouting him closely. A defensive cornerstone in the making.

Patrick Beach (Australia)

Herrington's compatriot and the goalkeeping sensation few saw coming.

Patrick Beach, 22, kept a clean sheet on his World Cup debut as Australia beat Türkiye 2-0. Source: AAP / Bob Frid/EPA

On his World Cup debut — controversially picked ahead of veteran captain Mat Ryan — the 22-year-old kept a clean sheet and made eight saves, including a full-stretch tip onto the post, in a stunning 2-0 win over Türkiye.

Beach became a household name overnight, and has already parlayed it into a five-year move from Melbourne City to newly promoted French first division side Troyes.

Warren Zaïre-Emery (France)

The 20-year-old PSG midfielder marshalled France's engine room while on the pitch, combining tireless pressing with a composure that belies his age.

Warren Zaïre-Emery, 20, featured for France on their run to the 2026 World Cup bronze-medal match. Source: AAP / Tom Weller/dpa/picture-alliance/Sipa USA

Already a two-time Champions League winner and serial Ligue 1 champion in Paris, he is touted as a future France captain — the natural successor to a storied generation of French midfielders.

Désiré Doué (France)

He made his mark whether starting or off the bench: a headed goal against Norway from a Bradley Barcola cross, and an assist for Mbappé against Morocco, across six lively appearances.

Désiré Doué, 21, was among France's brightest young talents at the 2026 World Cup. Source: AAP / Tom Weller/dpa/picture-alliance/Sipa USA

The 21-year-old PSG forward starred in the 2025 Champions League final, scoring twice in the win over Inter Milan, and arrived in North America off a 13-goal, 11-assist club season.

Two-footed, press-resistant and fearless, he is fast becoming undroppable for club and country.

Ayyoub Bouaddi (Morocco)

He anchored one of the tournament's great runs, starting all but one match as the Atlas Lions reached the quarter-finals for a second successive World Cup.

Ayyoub Bouaddi, 18, anchored Morocco's midfield as the Atlas Lions reached the 2026 World Cup quarter-finals. Source: AAP / Baptiste Fernandez/Icon Sport/Sipa USA

The opener against Brazil was his statement game, dictating play against Casemiro, Bruno Guimarães and Lucas Paquetá with 91 percent passing accuracy at just 18.

The Lille midfielder committed to Morocco over France only this year. Manchester City are now pushing hardest to sign him, ahead of Arsenal and Manchester United, with Lille holding out for around €100 million ($163 million). New City boss Enzo Maresca is reportedly keen to bring him straight into the Etihad squad rather than send him back on loan.

title: The World Cup is over. Here's where your football fix comes from next
text:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup might be over, but the beautiful game lives on, and there's plenty more football to sink your teeth into.

A cumulative 18 million Australians have tuned in to watch the World Cup over the course of the tournament, among them new fans who might be searching for a new team or a new hero player to follow.

Australia's top-flight A League men's competition will resume on 16 October.

Here's what's coming up before then.

EU and US comps underway and to come

As the northern hemisphere's summer draws to an end, players return to the fields and punters fill the stands in national competitions.

In Europe, Norway's Eliteserien is already in its sixth round and will run until December, with players such as Patrick Berg having almost no time to recover after making the World Cup quarter-finals in a historic run.

Fans hoping to get more of the charismatic Erling Haaland will have to wait until the English Premier League (EPL) kicks off on 21 August.

Haaland plays for Manchester City, along with the World Cup-winning Spanish captain Rodri, who was also awarded the tournament's best player.

Spain's La Liga starts on 15 August.

This star-studded comp is where you can watch 19-year-old Lamine Yamal, crucial goalscorer Ferran Torres, and Pedri take the field for Barcelona off the back of their iconic win against Argentina.

The Socceroos' Mat Ryan is expected to take the pitch with Levante while France's two-time World Cup Golden Boot winner, Kylian Mbappé, plays for Real Madrid.

The Italian Serie A and the French Ligue 1 will both start on 23 August.

In Italy we can expect to see Socceroos Alessandro Circati and Christian Volpato play for Parma and Sassuolo respectively.

The United States' Major League Soccer tournament has already started — tune in there to watch living legend Lionel Messi and fellow Argentine Rodrigo De Paul play for Inter Miami and Australia's Lucas Herrington make an impact with the Colorado Rapids.

Champions League

The European UEFA Champions League is considered the most prestigious annual tournament in international football.

The 36-team league is made up of the best clubs on the continent, and it's one way you can watch all of your World Cup favourites battle it out, even when they play in different national club competitions.

Qualifying rounds are already underway with the league phase starting 8 September.

Women's World Cup countdown begins

The 2026 FIFA World Cup is over, but the build-up to the 2027 Women's World Cup is underway.

Hosted by Brazil, the Matildas have already qualified for a spot in the July 2027 tournament while other qualifying matches are continuing this year.

Women's club competitions start in the US and Europe shortly after the local men's tournaments.

Many of the Matildas including Steph Catley, Kyra Cooney-Cross and Mary Fowler play in the English Women's Super League, which has its first match on 4 September.

Australian Championship

The second season of the Australian Championship is set to kick off in October, with eight foundation clubs and eight NPL premiers from across the country taking part in its second edition.

This is a tournament-style format where four groups of four teams will see the top two from each progress to a sudden-death finals series, with the Australian Championship trophy the grand prize.

title: WATCH: Super-sub Ferran Torres' goal wins Spain second World Cup
text:
In Brief Spain are the World Cup champions for the second time in the team's history.

Argentina have finished second after they were unable to put a score on the ledger.

Spain have seized the World Cup from the hands of defending champions Argentina after a late strike from Ferran Torres to seal a 1-0 victory in the New Jersey final.

Riding the momentum of their record-breaking fourth 2024 European Championship victory, Spain have etched their names into football history with a legacy win — taking home the trophy of all trophies for the second time, after their iconic 2010 World Cup triumph and making them the first nation in football history to hold the men's and women's World Cup titles at the same time.

The team remains the only nation ever to win the European Championship and World Cup in successive tournaments, having now done so a second time after their Euro 2024 title. They are also the first World Cup champion ever to concede only once across an entire tournament, and they are the first winner of the UEFA Nations League to capture a World title, having captured the eight-year-old competition in 2022-2023.

"We've won everything, everything," said Spain manager Luis de la Fuente through an interpreter. "And that's wonderful, with a generation of footballers, I'll say it again and again, who are an example for Spanish sport, for Spanish youth, for Spain. Together we are stronger, let there be no doubt about that."

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

For Argentina and skipper Lionel Messi — despite showing pluck and courage until the whistle — it was a somewhat disappointing end to a World Cup campaign that until this match showed they were full of surprises.

The Argentines had been dreaming of a fourth World Cup and a back-to-back trophy after winning against France at Qatar in 2022— but it was not to be.

Spain appeared to dominate for much of the game with Argentina barely getting a look in, but it was only in extra time that one of Spain's dozens of shots broke through.

In the 106th minute, well into extra time, Ferran Torres managed to land a strike into the roof of the net, just as a penalty shootout felt all but assured.

Torres was a late-game sub, taking the pitch in the 62nd minute.

"It was a goal scored by 47 million people, it wasn’t even mine or from the 26 players. It was destined to happen; it was ‌meant to be a winner," Torres said, speaking after the win.

It was 27C and sunny for the 80,000-strong crowd at New York New Jersey Stadium but it took close to full time for gameplay to heat up on the pitch.

After 49 minutes of bitsy, defensive play, the two sides were locked on 0-0 when they cleared the pitch for the half-time show. The score would not budge until well after the 90-minute mark.

As the clock wound down, chaos broke out around Spain's net — Messi taking back-to-back corners, Argentina making breathless attempt after attempt that they were unable to manoeuvre past Spain's formidable defensive line.

Spain dominated early, using their 67 per cent possession to show off their skills in the midfield and taunt Argentina, but the defending European champions only managed to convert three of those plays into attempts on goal going into the entertainment.

Around the five-minute mark, Lamine Yamal played a one-two in the box, collecting at the near post and getting a shot off that was saved by Emiliano Martinez.

Despite the early excitement, the first half wore on with few significant breaks. While Spain looked in control, neither side looked concerned.

After the first hydration break, Messi began dropping deeper and deeper into the midfield to pick up the play, only for Spain's defence to sweep him up every time.

The 39-year-old, eight-time Ballon d'Or winner Messi would have hoped for this final game to help him to at least nudge back into equal position with France's Kylian Mbappé as a top World Cup goal scorer.

Going into the match, the bowed-out Mbappé had 22 World Cup goals under his belt, compared to Messi's 21. But another wasn't to be, and with this largely rumoured to be his last time at the tournament, that is probably where his still mighty legacy will remain.

An apparent injury saw off Argentine centre-back Lisandro Martínez in the 43rd minute, to be replaced by Nicolás Otamendi.

The second half immediately marked a shift in play for both sides. When the teams returned from the not-quite-regulation 25-minute half-time break, they were both much livelier, but it wasn't enough to shift the game's play.

Some 43 seconds in, Spain's Alejandro Baena took an early chance on target — that was comfortably saved — before Argentina decided they had had enough of that and started taking more possession.

Despite more zest, the start-stop play that dominated the first half returned and Argentina remained defensive— the attacking side that came out of the gates swinging in the second half against England in the semi-final nowhere to be seen.

Spain continued to notch up attempts on goal, getting increasingly dangerous — but not dangerous enough — by attempt eight: A cross from Yamal offered Torres the chance to fling a header at speed into Martinez's waiting hands.

It offered a rare glimmer of excitement but it was not enough to change the pace as both teams headed into the tournament's last hydration break, increasingly tense and locked at nil-nil.

Off the back of the hydration break a frenzied feeling settled into play but not enough to change the score. Spain continued to make attempts on goal, but each one on target is saved without hesitation or fumble by Martínez.

Messi’s influence was almost completely negated time and time again by the well-organised and controlling Spanish midfield. Spain's midfield acted like a curtain until, in the 92nd minute — halfway through stoppage time— when Argentina made its first serious attack, only one of two it would make across the entire match.

As the game moved into extra time Argentina's side looked threadbare — down to just 10 men after Enzo Fernandez was sent off on a second yellow card; Messi the only attacker after Fernandez was sent off for an out-of-control tackle and Julian Alvarez came off in extra time.

The first goal of the game came well past the 90-minute mark, when Williams punted a defended ball into the net, only for it to be ruled out off the back of a penalty Mikel Merino incurred by standing on Nicolás Otamendi’s foot.

Nine minutes later, they nearly doubled their lead after Torres got a one-on-one with Martinez, but the offside flag was up, and it was thrown out.

Spain finished with 20 attempts, 12 on target, compared to Argentina's two off-target attempts.

Speaking after the game, Torres and his manager were candid about the game's slow evolution.

"Well, in the end, all finals are difficult, and when you have Messi on the opposing team, you always get nervous," Torres said.

"But ultimately, we've always depended on ourselves, on playing our style of football, and I think we demonstrated that once again today."

"Yes, I think the game should have been decided much earlier," de la Fuente added.

"Dibu's great saves prevented us from winning the game sooner. We could have taken a more comfortable lead. But this is a world championship final, and even with 10 men, you have to suffer at the end. But since we like to suffer, we've shown once again that we're ready for anything."

Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni admitted Spain were the better side but said he was proud of his side.

READ MORE All the records broken during the 2026 FIFA World Cup

"They were better, that’s the truth. But I’ll hold on to an enormous memory of what they’ve done, of what it’s worth to get this far," he said.

"We're great in victory, and we have to be great in defeat.

"Today we're showing that we know how to lose. We lost the match, and we're owning it.

"But that doesn’t mean we’ll stop remembering everything we did to get here.

Scaloni said he hadn't spoken with Messi about his future, but his tournament had been "unbelievable".

"He's 39 years old now," Scaloni said.

"I hope that everyone feels proud of him, of what he's achieved, because he's the best football player ever to set foot on a pitch. I have no doubt about this, and what he did during this World Cup was incredible. But also what he did before this."

— With additional reporting by Reuters news agency.

title: 'Impossible': Nauru community detainees say Australian funds not enough to buy food with
text:
IN BRIEF Community detainees in Nauru say they are not being given enough money by the Australian government to live on.

99 per cent of people interviewed said they don't have enough money for food.

Asylum seekers being detained within the community in Nauru are struggling to buy enough food and are being forced to choose between eating and paying for other necessities.

The difficulties are being driven by a mix of high living costs and low income support, according to a new report by the Asylum Seeker Resource Centre.

Of 78 people interviewed for the report, 99 per cent said they couldn't afford to buy enough food with their current allowance.

On top of that, 68 per cent said they rarely or never had access to fresh fruit, vegetables and meat, and 82 per cent said they didn't have easy access to free, clean drinking water.

Community detainees live on $260 a fortnight to cover all of their basic needs outside accommodation.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

The group have been sent to the tiny island nation under Australia's long-standing policy of offshore processing for asylum-seekers who arrive by boat.

Speaking anonymously, detainees consistently told the centre the allowance wasn't enough to sustain them, forcing them to decide between buying food and other necessities like clothing and internet access.

READ MORE Hundreds of former detainees to be deported to Nauru set to live among community

Many were running out of money before their next payment and were forced to reduce meals or go without food altogether.

"With food we have to compromise, if you buy vegetables you can't afford rice. If you buy meat, you can't afford fruit," one said.

"The money is not enough because everything is expensive here — rice, fish, we cannot buy any fruit here, internet for $100 per month, how is it possible to survive?" said another.

Nauru struggles with food insecurity due to its remote location and limited local agriculture, with perishable food flown in weekly and often selling out quickly or being prohibitively expensive.

With no rivers or freshwater lakes, the island also relies primarily on rainwater harvesting and desalination, meaning water shortages are common during times of low rainfall, forcing those who live there to turn to bottled water.

Asylum seekers in the community often struggle due to their lack of orientation or local knowledge as to how food systems work, as well as infrequent transportation to and from markets, the resource centre said.

As of 31 May, there were 101 people seeking asylum in Nauru, 91 of whom were in community accommodation.

The majority of those in detention in Nauru were young to middle-aged men from south and east Asia.

The group of asylum seekers are separate from a group of hundreds of former immigration detainees, known as the NZYQ cohort, sent to Nauru from Australia after the High Court in 2024 ruled indefinite detention to be unlawful.

title: Sydney ‘plyscraper’ and Atlassian’s new headquarters reaches new heights
text:
Advertisement

CultureArt & designCity life Sydney ‘plyscraper’ and Atlassian’s new headquarters reaches new heights Linda Morris July 22, 2026 — 12:04pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Some 39 storeys above Central Station, roads and rail lines radiate like scattered spaghetti between the Lego-like blocks of Sydney’s rooftops, extending to almost every point of the compass until meeting the blue horizon of ocean and harbour, and mountains. Far below, Mike Cannon-Brookes and his Atlassian co-founder Scott Farquhar were 25 years ago living in separate flat shares in Glebe and Randwick and building their software business. Up to half of the building could be leased to other businesses. Atlassian The physical manifestation of their ambitions, the $1.45 billion Atlassian Central tower, has structurally topped out. The core of what will be the world’s tallest hybrid timber building – dubbed the “plyscraper” – now stands at its highest point on the city skyline, built from a rare combination of mass timber, steel and concrete. The now solo tech entrepreneur joined NSW Treasurer Daniel Mookhey at the site on Wednesday morning to mark the milestone for Tech Central, Sydney’s technology precinct.

Advertisement

Loading “Sydney has always been our home and it’s one of our busiest offices anywhere, but in all these years we’ve never had a chance to build our own space from the ground up for how we work,” Cannon-Brookes said. As is traditional for topping-out ceremonies and as a gesture of good fortune for the build, an Australian native evergreen was lifted to the summit earlier this week to celebrate the placement of the final, highest structural beam. The tree recognises the work, skills and dedication of the tradespeople and construction crew and developers who brought the project to its maximum height. The landmark building officially eclipses the previous record holder, the 25-storey Ascent tower in Milwaukee in the United States, by 100 metres. Crown Towers is the city’s tallest habitable building at 272 metres. Mike Cannon-Brookes at the topping-out ceremony for the Atlassian building. James Brickwood

Advertisement

But those wings have been clipped along the way. Cannon-Brookes insists the software firm remains committed to its investment in its new headquarters despite recently reducing its workforce by 1600 employees. Related Article Exclusive

City life ‘TBC’: How $190 million was blown on Circular Quay revamp before the first sod is even turned Atlassian will serve as the chief anchor tenant with three of the habitats subleased to other tech ventures – a smaller footprint than first envisaged, but a move Atlassian says aligns with its mission to create a mecca for startups and innovators. “From the outset, we set a mission to have intelligence and sustainability baked into the building, not as an afterthought – which means better materials, better engineering, much better data and, in the end, a much better outcome for all concerned.” For property developer and co-owner Dexus, and builder Built, the next phase involves filling the steel exoskeleton with mass timber elements and a glass facade. Crews are installing central building services, with the development on track for practical completion by the end of this year.

Advertisement

Designed by local practice BVN in collaboration with New York-based firm SHoP Architects, the tower reconfigures traditional office tower spaces, where departments are confined to single floors on enclosed levels. BVN’s project director Tim Crawshaw said the building will extend to the top of a ring beam which is still to be cladded and varies in height due to the slope required for sun access into Prince Alfred Park. His collaborator Andrea Teixeira, associate for ShoP, said the design was seven years in development since its participation in the 2019 international design competition. Its global clients had been closely watching the project, especially the concept of the floating four floors of timber and its natural ventilation and landscape. Atlassian Central is the world’s tallest hybrid timber tower at more than 180 metres. James Brickwood She predicts this project will change people’s mind about working from home. “Here, they are connected to the city, they are not in a satellite village.” The new office spaces are arranged into interconnected four-storey clusters known as “habitats”, where three timber floors are arranged between steel-and-concrete mega floor plates.

Advertisement

Stacked within a surrounding steel exoskeleton – a structural approach similar to that of new Powerhouse Parramatta – these timber habitats eliminate the need for internal columns. Each pod features one fully air-conditioned floor and three floating lightweight timber floors attached to the steel-and-concrete core by revolutionary drag straps. These are recessed to overlook a triple-height atrium “park space” partially open to the elements. At street level, the base of the tower will incorporate a reconstructed historic Parcels Shed, which will be housing a 480-bed YHA youth hostel. In all, 10,000 cubic metres of timber, highly specialised and sourced from Austria, will go to the project, along with 8000 tonnes of structural steel and 30,000 cubic metres of green concrete. title: ‘I’m gonna die of this’: The hidden cost of former Socceroos captain’s greatest gift
text:
Advertisement

SportSoccerAustralian soccer ‘I’m gonna die of this’: The hidden cost of former Socceroos captain’s greatest gift Vince Rugari July 22, 2026 — 12:00pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

For as long as he can remember, former Socceroos captain Paul Wade has had a gift. When he was 11 years old, newly arrived in Australia from England, he realised he could head a football harder, cleaner and more accurately than the other kids he would play with. Paul Wade at his home on the Central Coast. Wolter Peeters “Goalkeepers always kicked the ball into the air back then. Nobody threw it out,” he said. “And because I was in midfield, I was probably one of the very few that was willing to head it. Right from the start, I knew I could head a ball - even if it was on the top of my head, where it hurt.

Advertisement

“I just felt that this was something I can do. And as I got older, the more I did it, the more accurate I got with my headers, the more people went: ‘Yeah, Wadey can head a ball. This is really cool.’ And then I just loved it.” Wade would go on to win 84 caps for the Socceroos, wear the captain’s armband more often than almost anyone, and build a reputation as one of Australia’s most fearless competitors. He retired in 1997, but continued to endear himself to generations of Australian soccer fans through his work in the media, becoming one of the game’s most recognisable faces and voices. Diego Maradona (right) and Socceroos captain Paul Wade walk onto the Sydney Football Stadium for the first leg of a World Cup qualifying tie in 1993. Getty Not that he thought much about it at the time, but Wade’s willingness to put his body - and his head - on the line was the foundation for his legendary career. Every time he was split open by a stray elbow in an aerial contest, Wade could feel the adulation from supporters, teammates and coaches, and it made him walk taller. “It was a badge of honour. And I fed off it,” he said.

Advertisement

“They cheered for me and they had respect for me when I went back into the dressing rooms. And for the rest of my career, I didn’t mind getting it done: stitches at halftime, coming off concussed and then saying, ‘Right boss, I’m ready to go back on.’” Wade, now 64, cites the motto that he and so many footballers of his vintage used to live by: never let them know you’re hurt. Former Socceroos captain Paul Wade has been diagnosed with probable CTE. Wolter Peeters “That’s what kept me out there,” he said. “And it didn’t matter what happened, who hit me, if I fell on somebody - I didn’t make it an issue.” Little did he know his greatest attribute would cause him so much grief later in life.

Advertisement

These days, Wade’s memory is not as good as it once was. It has never been great: he was diagnosed with epilepsy during his playing career and underwent brain surgery in 2002 to remove a scarred section of his temporal lobe, and that history made it easier for him to dismiss the concerns of those closest to him whenever he forgot an appointment, or couldn’t find something he had put down only 30 seconds earlier. Related Article Concussion crisis ‘My brain was smashed’: Knocked unconscious for nine minutes, this ex-Socceroo is living a nightmare But there were also changes to Wade’s behaviour which didn’t track with the person they knew. Eventually, those changes became serious enough for his family and friends to intervene. “There were a couple of incidents where Paul’s behaviour was not appropriate,” said his wife, Val. “And when I say not appropriate … he spoke to people and did things that were just not OK. We nearly lost a friendship over it. That was the turning point. That’s when we sat him down and said, ‘You’ve got to go and see a neurologist. There’s something wrong, this is not you.’

Advertisement

“It still took probably a year to get him to do it, because he still didn’t think that there was something wrong with him, and he just kept putting it off.” After speaking to his GP, Val began researching his symptoms and saw signs consistent with CTE, or chronic traumatic encephalopathy, a degenerative brain disease associated with prolonged exposure to repeated head impacts. Definitive CTE diagnoses can only be made after death. But late last year, Wade was told by Dr Rowena Mobbs, one of Australia’s leading concussion experts, that he had probable CTE - the highest level of diagnostic certainty possible in a living patient, based on his history as a footballer, neurological symptoms and specialist assessment. Wade can’t point to a single, major concussion incident which changed everything for him, like his former Socceroos teammate Warren Spink. But CTE is also associated with prolonged exposure to repeated head impacts, including sub-concussive blows that may not produce immediate or obvious symptoms. In football, that has focused attention on the cumulative effect of heading the ball over the course of a career, or lifetime. “When I found out, the girls [his daughters] were very concerned. I said, ‘Don’t worry. I haven’t changed. It’s just that they’ve given me a headline,’” Wade said.

Advertisement

Related Article Analysis

FIFA World Cup Spanish domination shows the limitations of ‘suffering’ in football “But when the neurologist said, ‘It’s terminal. Go and make some memories,’ that’s when reality kicks in. All my mates have been going, ‘Yeah, but we’re all terminal, Wadey.’ But I’ll never forget those words when she said them, that it’s terminal. “I’m gonna die of this. I might not die for 20 years, I might get hit by a bus, but there is no cure. And that was the moment I thought, people have got to understand … you don’t have to have a concussion to have CTE.” Wade doesn’t blame anyone and has no regrets. That’s just what he and so many others who loved football did, and still do. But he is hopeful that, by going public with his story, greater awareness of the dangers of heading the ball can be raised, and the risks can be mitigated. England has prohibited deliberate heading in matches for children aged under 12, while limiting heading in training for older junior age groups. Mobbs wants Australia to introduce similar restrictions, and Wade would also like to see that happen.

Advertisement

“I just want people to know that if you want to continue to do thousands of headers, these could be the consequences,” Wade said. “And for those who are making the rules or should be applying the protocols, do it. Do it now. Don’t think about the consequences of that if you can save a few thousand lives. “Because if I die in the next five years, all those doubters will go, ‘Wow, we’ve just wasted five years.’” Loading

title: Cruise boat captain sacked after sleeping in front of passengers
text:
Advertisement

BusinessCompaniesUnfair dismissal Cruise boat captain sacked after sleeping in front of passengers Millie Muroi July 22, 2026 — 11:59am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

A cruise tour boat captain has failed to win back his job after being sacked for unsafe and “cavalier” behaviour including sleeping and snoring in front of guests and leaving unqualified staff to steer the boat. The man, who worked for Queensland business SeaLink Whitsundays on their “Wildcat” vessels from June 2024 to June 2025, was employed as a casual master — responsible for the safe operation of the boat — and was found by the Fair Work Commission to have been validly dismissed. The cruise captain was found to have dumped food into the water while guests were snorkelling nearby. In June last year, one of SeaLink’s deckhands made a complaint about “very unsafe behaviour” from a “current skipper within the company”. The allegations included the man sleeping on the vessel and snoring in front of guests to the point where they asked “what’s wrong with the skipper?”, constantly vaping on board, and making other crew members drive the boat despite their lack of qualification.

Advertisement

Related Article Unfair dismissal ‘Evasive’ postie who drove to KFC on lunch break loses job Several other staff deckhands also gave evidence against the man. Fair Work Commission deputy president Nicholas Lake said that during the hearing, while the man was “perhaps not deliberately dishonest”, he often deflected and blamed others when asked about his own behaviour. “He appeared to enjoy the theatrics of being on the witness stand,” Lake said, adding the man tried to dismiss the evidence of the young women involved in the investigation, calling them “a junior group of girls who have nothing better to do than gossip”. The man also compared a girls-only group chat for women working on the boat to the movie Mean Girls.

Advertisement

Lake said the man’s suggestion that the witnesses were less reliable because they were allegedly gossipy young women was “condescending at best and sexist at worst” and noted he was the least credible witness of all the witnesses who gave evidence. The man admitted to falling asleep on multiple occasions but said it was common practice in some instances such as when the boat was moored and guests were on the beach, and that in another instance, he had taken sleeping tablets before his shift and in retrospect should not have gone to work that day. The man worked for Queensland business SeaLink Whitsundays on their “Wildcat” vessels from June 2024, was employed as a casual master Getty Images/iStockphoto The man also admitted he vaped on board occasionally, but said he did it to help him quit smoking after seeing a hypnotherapist. One deckhand alleged the man said, “I just don’t give a f---, let them die,” in response to a conversation about snorkel control measures, but he denied this and the commission found there was inadequate evidence to prove this had occurred.

Advertisement

Related Article Unfair dismissal Chauffeur who agreed to retire at 65 to be paid $40,000 after ageist dismissal The commission also said the man asked his deckhands to take the helm without supervision despite them not having the appropriate qualifications to drive the vessel, although the man argued he hadn’t put them “in control” of the boat. “They were following direct orders from me to drive in a straight line,” he said. “There were no manoeuvres, they were not asked to change direction, they were not asked to change engine speeds.” The man admitted he had thrown a tray of rice salad overboard during one trip out of frustration with the crew and acknowledged this was an error in judgment as it breached marine pollution regulations. However, one witness alleged it was a more significant amount of food including bread rolls, meats and salads, thrown into the water while guests were snorkelling, and that the man had said he did not want to carry heavy bins of food at the end of the day. The man argued that his conduct amounted to “a number of rather innocuous indiscretions”, but the commission ultimately found there was a pattern of inappropriate behaviour.

Advertisement

However, the commission noted there was some doubt about whether the man had a fair opportunity to respond to the company’s allegations before a conclusion had been reached on them and to have his say on whether the conduct warranted dismissal. “I consider the rushed and appended disciplinary process in this matter was unreasonable and, as a result, caused some unfairness,” Lake said, ordering $5000 in compensation to be paid to the man. title: Miners and energy stocks push ASX higher; Lynas, tech stocks decline
text:
Advertisement

Updated BusinessMarketsWorld markets Miners and energy stocks push ASX higher; Lynas, tech stocks decline Staff writers Updated July 22, 2026 — 11:58am ,first published July 22, 2026 — 5:13am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

The Australian sharemarket advanced in early trade on Wednesday after Wall Street was pushed higher by gains in makers of computer chips and other winners of the artificial-intelligence boom, shrugging off another jump in oil prices amid the hostilities in the Middle East. The S&P/ASX 200 was up 25.3 points, or 0.3 per cent, at 8818.60 as of 11.22am AEST, boosted by the energy and mining sectors. The local bourse closed flat on Tuesday. The Australian dollar was trading at US$70.07. AI stocks once again were at the centre of the action on Wall Street, and they rose for a second straight day after tumbling the week before. AP Energy and materials stocks pushed the ASX higher in early trade, thanks to higher prices for oil, copper and gold overnight. Brent crude oil briefly got near $US92 per barrel for the first time in more than five weeks because of continued attacks between the US and Iran. It later pared its gain to 2 per cent and settled at $US91.01. That’s up from less than $US72 in early July, which is roughly where it was before the war with Iran. US President Donald Trump played down the prospect of immediate talks with Iran as the two sides exchanged strikes near the Strait of Hormuz, while Houthi militants in Yemen threatened shipping in the Red Sea.

Advertisement

Local oil giants Woodside and Santos rose in early trade, up 0.6 and 0.8 per cent, respectively, while local refiners Ampol and Viva Energy both added 1.2 per cent. Coal producer Yancoal gained 1.7 per cent as higher oil prices boost demand for coal as a fossil fuel alternative. Mining heavyweights BHP, Rio Tinto and Fortescue jumped 3 per cent, 2.4 per cent and 2 per cent, respectively, after copper futures jumped 3.3 per cent. After demand for iron ore peaked, the mining giants have pivoted to the red metal to become their main earnings driver. Related Article Opinion

Online shopping $900 million fines look like the cost of doing business for giant shopping apps Elizabeth Knight Business columnist Meanwhile, gold prices held their gains as traders monitored the war-induced threats to energy supply routes that risk stoking inflation and putting pressure on the Federal Reserve to hike interest rates. Bullion was trading at about $US4080 an ounce, after gaining almost 2 per cent the previous session. Northern Star Resources rose 2.5 per cent, while Evolution Mining jumped 4.2 per cent. South32 climbed a further 3.7 per cent, adding to its 6.6 per cent rally on Tuesday. The mining outlier this morning was Lynas Rare Earths, which slumped 6.2 per cent after the company revealed in a trading update a more than 10 per cent slump in its output of NdPR – an important alloy blend of rare earths elements crucial for making the strong magnets that power the traction motors used in electric cars and wind turbines – in the June quarter compared to a year ago, due to issues at its new Mount Weld water recycling plants and ore concentrate quality issues.

Advertisement

While rising oil prices boosted energy stocks, the flip side is threatening a re-acceleration of inflation, which in turn could push the US Federal Reserve and other central banks to raise interest rates, slowing economies and undercutting prices for stocks and other investments. Real estate investment trusts, which tend to suffer when rising rates boost the returns of bonds, were trading lower, with warehouse and AI data centre owner Goodman Group down 1.2 per cent, Westfield shopping centres landlord Scentre down 0.5 per cent and Vicinity down 1.1 per cent. Healthcare stocks also declined, with biotech giant CSL down 1.9 per cent and Chemist Warehouse owner Sigma Healthcare slipping 1.42 per cent. Tech stocks gave back their gains from Tuesday, with software makers bearing the brunt of the losses. Xero, the nation’s biggest tech stock, lost 4 per cent. WiseTech fell 2 per cent after saying it bought a developer of AI-powered supply chain risk and compliance intelligence technology for $10 million and shares. Technology One slid 3 per cent. Wesfarmers slipped 0.7 per cent after the conglomerate said it would spend between $645 million and $715 million to expand the Mount Holland lithium mine and concentrator with its partner, Sociedad Quimica y Minera de Chile. It will pay for the investment using existing cash and debt facilities.

Advertisement

The big four banks were slightly higher in early trade. CBA was up 0.3 per cent, Westpac and National Australia Bank both rose 0.4 per cent and ANZ Bank edged up 0.1 per cent. Related Article Opinion

Trade wars Trump wants to make his trade wall bigger and better than ever before Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist On Wall Street overnight, the S&P 500 climbed 0.9 per cent. The Dow Jones added 385 points, or 0.7 per cent, and the Nasdaq composite rose 1.3 per cent. AI stocks once again were at the centre of the action, and they rose for a second straight day after tumbling the week before. After rocketing higher because of the boom in investment in AI chips and data centres, they’ve come under pressure in recent weeks on worries that they shot too high. Concerns are also weighing that investment in AI may fall off if it doesn’t produce as much profit as hoped.

Advertisement

Micron Technology jumped 12.2 per cent and added to its 1.9 per cent gain from the day before, coming off its 13.3 per cent drop from last week. Nvidia added 2 per cent; they were the two strongest forces lifting the S&P 500. On Wall Street, several stronger-than-expected profit reports from big US companies also helped the market higher. 3M climbed 7.3 per cent after topping analysts’ expectations for both profit and revenue in the latest quarter. It also raised its forecast for profit over the full year of 2026. Hasbro rallied 8.8 per cent after the toy maker said its Magic: The Gathering game topped $US500 million in revenue for a quarter for the first time. It also raised its revenue forecast for the year. General Motors cruised 4.9 per cent higher after the automaker’s profit and revenue for the latest quarter beat analysts’ expectations and CEO Mary Barra said demand in North America remains strong. Alphabet slid 1.5 per cent after Google released a trio of cheaper versions of its Gemini AI model, but shared no timing updates for the flagship Pro model that has already delayed its launch by several weeks.

title: The real oil shock from the Iran war might be just beginning
text:
Advertisement

BusinessMarketsMiddle East at war Opinion The real oil shock from the Iran war might be just beginning Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist July 22, 2026 — 11:57am

July 22, 2026 — 11:57am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

A new front has opened in the war in the Middle East, global oil and refined product inventories are tumbling and oil and gasoline prices are spiking again. So much for all that winning. After the 60-day ceasefire agreement was signed by the United States and Iran last month and the Strait of Hormuz reopened, shipments of crude oil from the Gulf recommenced and oil and gasoline prices fell sharply, with Brent crude trading (briefly) below $US70 a barrel. Maritime traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has ground to a halt yet again, raising concerns of another supply shock. AP The oil market, which had been helped by the release of about 300 million barrels from strategic reserves and China’s halving of its imports, appeared to be recovering rapidly. Global oil inventories rose by 21 million barrels in June, the first increase since the outbreak of the war in February. The breakdown of the ceasefire late last month initially had only a modest impact on oil prices, which edged up towards, and then above, $US80 a barrel as the hostilities resumed and intensified.

Advertisement

On Monday, however, with the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed again, the Iran-aligned Houthis emailed most of the world’s major shipping companies, threatening to attack ships attempting to export oil from Red Sea ports via the Bab el-Mandeb strait. The Saudis have been using their pipelines and their port at Yanbu to ship about 4 million barrels a day – compared with only about a million barrels a day before the war broke out – via the Red Sea, with about 2.5 million barrels a day heading south, via the Bab el-Mandeb, to Asian buyers and the rest travelling the far longer route via the Suez Canal (which can’t handle the larger tankers) at the northern end of the sea. The oil price immediately broke through $US90 a barrel. The world is now far more vulnerable than it was in the earlier phase of the war.

Advertisement

The International Energy Agency’s July oil market report said that about three quarters of the 400 million barrels of strategic oil reserves that its members agreed to release in March have been released. What’s left of that commitment could be exhausted within weeks. The oil shock definitely isn’t over and, indeed, with no obvious way for the US to gracefully retreat from the conflict it chose to start, the real shock might just be getting underway. The ceasefire did produce a surge in oil “on the water” for the first time since February, but it also produced a revival in demand, which had previously been reduced as the war pushed up prices. Both OECD and non-OECD oil stocks fell again last month – by a combined total of roughly 100 million barrels – and Washington’s strategic reserve has fallen to its lowest level in more than 40 years. More threatening is the position in refined products. Businesses and consumers don’t consume oil, but its derivatives like petrol, diesel jet fuels and fertilisers.

Advertisement

The margins on refined products hit four-year highs earlier this month, even when crude was trading just above $US70 a barrel. Exports from the Persian Gulf of refined products were less than half their prewar levels last month, even as crude flows were running at about 75 per cent of prewar levels again. That disparity is partly due to the combination of Iranian attacks on Middle East refineries and the depletion of their inventories. But stocks have also been hit by the success of Ukraine’s targeting of Russian refineries and oil industry infrastructure, which has forced Russia – previously the world’s second-largest exporter of diesel – not just to halt exports of diesel refined products, but to start importing them. That has subtracted supply and added demand to the market for refined products, as has a shift in China’s energy security strategy. During the war, China nearly halved its imports of crude, taking about 5 million barrels a day of demand out of the market while restricting exports of refined products.

Advertisement

With a strategic reserve of its own estimated at more than 1.2 billion barrels, it was better positioned than any other economy to ride out the war. With the ceasefire, however, it resumed purchases of oil to rebuild its depleted reserve. Related Article Updated

Middle East at war Trump to bomb Iran’s Pickaxe Mountain nuclear site ‘soon’, appears to blame Jordan for US deaths The released strategic reserves and China’s withdrawal from the market are probably the two major factors in oil prices failing to reach the $US150-a-barrel prices that were predicted early in the conflict, which the IEA described as the biggest energy shock in history. The price peaked at about $US126 a barrel in late April. Those buffers, however, have been run down and, with the Strait of Hormuz again closed and the Houthis threatening to shut the Bab el-Mandeb, supply is under an even more significant threat than it was during the first phase of the conflict. The most acute threat to the global economy remains the supply of refined product.

Advertisement

Gasoline and diesel inventories around the world are at or close to record lows – US diesel stocks are, for instance, at 20-year lows and gasoline stocks at their lowest level since 2012 – and global refining output, which didn’t surge like crude oil flows during the ceasefire, is around 5 million barrels a day lower than it was a year ago. An oil tanker struck by the Houthis in the Red Sea in 2024. The rebel group is threatening to strike tankers attempting to export oil from Red Sea ports via the Bab el-Mandeb strait. AP The US had boosted exports of refined products to record levels (with record profits for US refiners) as Middle East supply fell away, but the decline in domestic stocks has seen its refiners’ output redirected to the domestic market, with export volumes falling back quite sharply. With no obvious ability to bring back refined product supply to prewar levels quickly while the conflict continues – the damaged refineries in the Middle East and Russia will take months, at least, to repair and ramp up their output – it is demand destruction, driven by price spikes, that will have to drive a rebalancing of the supply-demand equation. Ominously for US President Donald Trump’s Republicans facing midterm elections in November, US petrol prices rose back above $US4 a gallon this week. This time last year, the average US petrol price was $US3.14 a gallon. Diesel is selling at about $US5.14 a gallon, compared to $US3.72 a year ago.

Advertisement

Related Article Oil These are the corporate gamblers who never waste a good energy crisis The oil shock – and more particularly the downstream effects of that shock on refined product prices and supply – definitely isn’t over and, indeed, with no obvious way for the US to gracefully retreat from the conflict it chose to start, the real shock might just be getting underway. That has implications for global economic activity and for inflation rates around the world. The drop in the US inflation last month from 4.2 per cent to 3.5 per cent as the ceasefire took hold and oil and gasoline prices fell illustrated how significant the impact of the war has been on businesses and households. Trump, of course, says he isn’t worried about the escalation and broadening of Middle East hostilities, even as he professed no interest in restarting negotiations with Iran.

Advertisement

And if the Houthis do close the Red Sea? “If something like that happens, we take care of it,” he said. “We’ll take care of it”: Donald Trump says he isn’t worried about the escalation and broadening of Middle East hostilities. AP The US has done such a great job of taking care of the Strait of Hormuz that less than a handful of tankers now risk the passage each day. “If we left tomorrow, we had a big, we had a big success. But we’re not leaving tomorrow,” Trump said this week. Trump defines success and winning differently to the rest of us.

Advertisement

Every day that the two straits remain closed and oil and refined product prices stay elevated underscores how unsuccessful – and humiliating – Trump’s excursion in the Middle East has been, and how costly it is – not just for the US, but for the rest of the world.
